## FILING INSTRUCTIONS

A. Requirements. The original application, general submission requirements (listed below), and other
requirementsasoutlinedinthe appropriate checklist (see item B), either typedorlegibly printed,
together with all supporting documents mustbefiled with the Administrative Officerinaccordance with

Chapter 185, Article XXVI of the Town Code, the Zoning Ordinance.

## 1) A letterofeligibility issuedbythe Rhode Island Housing Mortgage Finance Corporation, orinthe caseofprojects primarily fundedbythe U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Developmentorother stateorfederal
agencies, an award letter indicating the subsidyorapplicationinsuch formasmaybeprescribed foramunicipal

government subsidy
## 2) A letter signedbythe authorized representativeofthe applicant, setting forth the specific sections and

provisionsofapplicable local ordinances and regulations from which the applicantisseeking adjustments.
## 3) A proposed timetable for the commencementofconstruction and completionofthe project;

## 4) Those items includedinthe checklist for preliminary plan reviewinthe Land Development and
Subdivision Regulations, § 200-1 et seq., except for evidenceofstateorfederal permits.

PLEASE NOTE: Notwithstanding the submission requirements set forth above, the permitting authority may
request additional, reasonable documentation throughout the public hearing, including, but not limited to,
opinionsofexperts, credible evidenceofapplication for necessary federal andorstate permits, and advice from

other local boards and officials.

B. Submission Requirements Specific to Applications Based Upon Their Classification.

In additiontothe items above, submission requirements shall include the following items basedonthe
classificationofthe applicationasdeterminedbythe Administrative Officer. All required state and federal
permits mustbeobtained priortorecordingofthe approved plan and/or the issuanceofa building permit.

Dear Charles:

We have received your application foraletterofeligibilitytopursue a Comprehensive Permitinthe Town of Barrington. You are proposing a 344-unit condominium development that wouldbecomprised of:

a. Highty-Six (86) affordable units that willbesoldtohouseholds earninguptoamaximum of °
120% of the Area Median Income (“AMI”). (Refer to “Exhibit A” for unit breakdowns)
b. Two Hundred Fifty-Eight (258) market-rate units. (Refer to “Exhibit A” for unit breakdowns)

Basedonthe current AMI, the Freddie Mac Primary Mortgage Market Survey 30-year fixed-rate
mortgage average interest rate (6.76%) asofthe dateofthis letter, anticipated taxes, insurance,
and mortgage insurance costs, the maximum sales prices for affordable condominium unit withacondo fee of $250.00, priced for households earning 120% of the AMI are outlined in Exhibit
A. The maximum permitted sales prices may changeinthe future duetochangesinanyofthe

above-noted purchase price inputs.

In additiontorequirements for affordability and monitoring under R.I.G.L. 45-53, the
constructionotrehabilitationofqualifying low-and-moderate income housing units mustbeassistedbya state, federal, or municipal subsidy program.

Afterapreliminary reviewofthe plans and financial assumptions for the proposed sale of Two
Hundred Fifty-Fight (258) market-rate units and Eighty-Six (86) affordable unitstohouseholds
earning 120% of the AMI, alltobe located at 33 Middle Highway, Plat 14 and Lot 2, 4, 13, 341,
342, 343 in the Town of Bartington and approximately 2.2 Acres located in East Providence, Map

613 Block 6 Lot 22, RIHousing has determined that:

e This project appearstobe eligible foramunicipal subsidy under R.I.G.L. Title 45 Towns
and Cities, Chapter 45-53 Low- and Moderate-Income Housing, Section 45-53-4

RE: Proposed Land Redevelopment Project
Belton Court Village
Middle Highway, Barrington, RI

Dear Mr. Kalocsay:

Crossman Engineering, in accordance with our scopeofservices, has completedatraffic impact study foraproposed residential redevelopment projectinthe Town of Barrington, Rhode Island. The siteislocatedtothe immediate north of County Road (Route 103) onalarge property formerly utilized for institutional
purposesbythe Barrington College and most recentlybythe Zion Bible Institute. The 39 plus acre parcelispartially developed with multiple buildings, including the historic Be/ton Court structure. Manyofthe
college buildings that wereindisrepair have recently been razed leaving the parcel primarily wooded with
areasofdisturbed open space.

Based upon our discussions, itisour understanding thatanexisting partially developed lot willberedevelopedtoincludeamixtureofresidential buildings including single family homes, townhouses and
larger multi-unit buildings. A small neighborhood community space and retail componentisalso proposedtoprovide services for the residential village environment. Accesstothe property willbeprovided from
three site access roads intersecting with Middle Highway. The primary entrancetothe new neighborhood
willbelocatedatthe existing driveway intersection opposite Primrose Hill Road that willbemodifiedtoaccommodate site changesinthis immediate area.

The study included herein, was conductedtodetermine the adequacyofthe existing servicing roadwaystoaccommodate anticipated traffictobe generatedbythe residential redevelopment project. An analysisofpotential impactstothe roadway capacity and safety has been completed andisdiscussedinthe
following report.

## 1.0 INTRODUCTION

The objectiveofthe following studyisto assess the potential traffic impacts associated withaproposed
redevelopment projectinthe Town of Barrington, Rhode Island. The existing 39.1-acre site, whichisa
remnantofthe former Peck Estate, consistsofsix lots containing approximately 36.9 acresofland in
Barrington and 2.2 acresinthe City of East Providence. Refer to Figure 1 on the following page for project
locationinthe community. The Peck Estate purchased from Massasoit by Joseph Peckinthe 1600’s,
encompassed over 800 acres between the Taunton River and Narragansett Bay. The portionofland within
the Town of Barrington, was known as Ousamequin Farm and was actively used for farming purposes
during the early years. The property remainedasancestral land ownedbythe prominent Peck family that
contributed substantiallytothe town during this period, until the 1940’s when Frederick Peck donated
the current 39-acre site and associated buildingstothe Providence Homeopathic Hospital for medical use.
Other portionsofthe expansive property along Primrose Hill Road and Middle Highway, were subdivided
off for residential housing development which now surrounds the property, and 42 acres along Hundred
Acre Cove were preservedbythe Town of Barringtontocreate the Ousamequin Nature Trails and Bird
Sanctuary.

There are multiple buildingsonthe site that were constructed during different periodsofthe early 1900’s
including the historic stone mansion buildings known as Be/ton Courtbuilt in 1906 and expanded in 1927.
Other newer buildings were later constructedtorepurpose the property for institutional use that included
the Barrington College and Zion Bible Institute. Manyofthe newer class and dormitory buildings that
were built for the educational facilities have been removed over the last three years duetotheir poor
condition and incompatibility with the proposed residential reuseofthe site.

Itisthe intentofthe projecttorenovateaportionofthe stone mansion for reuseasprimarilyaneighborhood public space for the village residents, andtoconstructamixtureofresidential unit types
from single family cottagestomultiunit condominium buildingsina village environment. Primary accesstothe new neighborhood, Belton Court Village, willbeprovided on Middle Highwayatthe existing
driveway entranceatthe Primrose Hill Road intersection. Secondary access roads intersecting with
Middle Highway are proposedattwo locations along the property frontage.

The study summarized herein focusedonboth traffic flow efficiency and safety along Middle Highway,
County Road and Primrose Hill Roadinthe immediate vicinityofthe subject property, including their
intersections. The potential impacts associated with the site related traffic have been defined and
evaluatedinaccordance with standard traffic engineering guidelines and procedures.

The traffic engineering study completed for this project included the following:

¢ A traffic counting programtodefine the existing traffic patterns and operational characteristics
along the servicing roadways. The data collection included an Automatic Traffic Recorder (ATR)
counts on Middle Highway and Primrose Hill Roadinthe vicinityofthe subject property and peak
hour Turning Movement Counts (TMC) at the Middle Highway intersections with County Road and

## 2.0 PROJECT AREA

As notedinthe previous section, the proposed redevelopment project situatedonthe former Zion Bible
Institute property, will include constructionofa mixtureofresidential housing unitsina village style
environment. The site will containatotal of 344 residential units, a percentageofwhich willbereserved
for senior housing. Additionally, the west wingofthe stone manner containing approximately 20,000
square feetofavailable building area, willberepurposed predominately for common space
accommodating neighborhood activities and management services for the village, withasmall 1,500
square foot commercial element that wouldbefocusedona small neighborhood business use. Figure 2
on the following page depicts the general project area, and the boundary linesofthe subject property.

Land useinthe immediate area canbedefinedasa predominately residential properties immediately
surrounding the subject property and along Middle Highway and Primrose Hill Road. Furthertothe south
there are recreational and institutional uses suchasthe Red Brick preschool, Primrose Hill Elementary
School and St. James Church. The Sherwood baseball field complexissituated off of County Road adjacenttothe church.

Middle Highway, County Road and Primrose Hill Road will serveasthe primary access routestothe site.
Based upon the good operating characteristics along these servicing roadways, and the estimated hourly
volume and typeoftraffic associated with the redevelopment project, a study impact area was defined.
The limitsofthe study area for analysis focusedonthese roadwaysinthe immediate site vicinity including
their intersections.

## 3.0 EXISTING CONDITIONS

## 3.1 ROADWAYS

Middle Highway

Middle Highway, a state-maintained roadway, runs north-south from Hickory Lane/Ormand Drive to
Nayatt Road, a distance of 2.5 miles withavarying cross section and geometric features along its length.
North of County Road (RI 103), Middle Highway classifiedasan urban major collector and south of County
Road (RI 103) itisclassifiedasan urban minor arterial. The roadway, posted at 25-mph, operatesasa
major north-south link through the community, paralleling both Route 114 to the east and Washington
Roadtothe west.

In the project area, Middle Highwaytothe south of Primrose Hill Roadisa two-lane road approximately
26 feet wide consistingoftwo 12-foot travel lanes and 1-foot shoulders delineated withadouble yellow
centerline and white shoulder markings. To the north of Primrose Hill Road, Middle Highwayisvariableinwidth 24 to 30 feet, andisdelineated with narrower travel lanes. For muchofits length this sectionofroadway is 30 feet wide consisting of 10-foot travel lanesineach direction. The shoulder widthisgenerally
2 feetonthe westerly sideofthe road withagreater 8-foot widthonthe easterly side.

The wide shoulder was constructed several years ago when this sectionofroadway was resurfaced. An
existing bituminous sidewalkonthe easterly sideofthe road was reconfiguredtoallow constructionofthe wider shoulder/sidewalk area withaberm insteadofbituminous curbing, accommodating both
pedestrians and bicyclist. To the south of Primrose Hill Road, an offset bituminous sidewalkinpoor
conditionisprovided along the easterly sideofthe road extending to Edgewood Drive. Thereisa short
sectionofsidewalk missing between Edgewood Drive and the signalized intersection with County Road
(RI 103) that provides concrete sidewalksoneach cornerofthe intersection linking signal-controlled
crosswalks. There arenosidewalks along the westerly sideofthe corridor north of County Road.

The town/state should investigate the feasibilityofinstalling the missing short section(s) of sidewalk along
the easterly sideofthe roadinordertoprovideacontinuous pedestrian route along Middle Highwayinthis area. Further south the Rhode Island Department of Transportation recently installed concrete
sidewalks along the westerly side of Middle Highway from Sherwood Lane south, past Primrose
Elementary School and the Barrington Middle Schoo! to the area of Maple Avenue, linkingtothe East Bay
Bike Pathatits southern terminus. The sidewalk was installedaspartofa Safe Routes to School efforttoenhance pedestrian safety and accessinthe vicinity of, and along routestolocal schools. In the project
area, thereisone short missing section between County Road south to Sherwood Lane, similartowhatismissingonthe north sideofthe County Road intersection.

The pavement surface canbeclassifiedasbeingingood conditiontothe northasit was recently
resurfaced, buttothe south of Primrose Hill Roaditcanbeclassifiedasbeinginfair condition withafew
limited areasofpavementinpoor condition with distress causedbypoor drainage. There are small
sectionsofbituminous berm and concrete curb situated sporadically along both sidesofthe road to

stormwater runoff was evident. There are utility poles with street lighting locatedonthe westerly sideofthe corridor from the County Road (RI 103) areatothe Primrose Hill Road intersection. Northofthe

of Middle Roadinthe vicinity of

County Road (Route 103)

There are two distinct sections of
County Road (RI 103) extending
through the Town of Barrington.
The primary route runs north-south
from RI 114 at Wampanoag Trail
through the centeroftown to Sowams Roadatthe Warren Bridge, a distance of 2.7 miles. The second
section of County Road, in the general project area, runsinan east-west orientation extending from State
Route 114 to Washington Road and Willett Avenue, a distance of 0.7 miles. The east/west roadway within
the project areaisposted at 30 mph andisclassifiedasan urban principal arterial.

In the vicinityofthe Middle Highway signalized intersection, County Road (RI 103) on the east extending
to Route 114 is 26 feet wide consisting of 12-foot travel lanes and one-foot shouldersineach direction.
To the westofthe signalized intersection the two-lane roadway widens to 38 feet and provides for 13-
foot travel lanes and 6-foot shoulders delineated withadouble yellow centerline and white shoulder
markings. There arenosidewalks along the corridorinthe project area except for the aforementioned
short areaofsidewalk with curbingateach cornerofthe Middle Highway/County Road (RI 103)
intersection. The pavement surface canbeclassifiedasbeingingoodtofair condition. Within the project
limits, cobra-head light fixturesonutility poles are locatedonthe north sideofthe streettothe east of
Middle Highway andonthe south sideofthe streettothe west of Middle Highway.

Primrose Hill Road

Primrose Hill Roadisshort urban major collector road running east-west between Wampanoag Trail (RI
114) and Middle Highway, a distance of 0.4 miles. Primrose Hill Road, posted at 25 mphisstop-controlledonthe westbound approach to Middle Highway andonthe eastbound approach to Wampanoag Trail. In
the project area, the road measures 26 feetinwidth consistingofan 11-foot travel lane and 2-foot
shoulderineach direction delineatedbya double yellow centerline and white shoulder markings.
Bituminous berm extends along both sidesofthe roadway andnosidewalks are provided.

## 3.2 INTERSECTIONS

Middle Highway at County Road (RI 103)

Middle Highway intersects County Roadtoformasignalized, four-way junction. All approachestothe
intersection provideasingle all-purpose lane. The traffic signal system was upgraded several years ago
along with pavement, curbing and sidewalk/curb ramps, and pavement markings. High visibility marked
crosswalks were installed across all legsofthe intersection including curb ramps and pedestrian signalstoenhance pedestrian safetyatthe intersection.

The traffic signal equipmentisin good condition having been installedinthe last decade, as previously
noted. The layoutofthe signal equipment consistsofsingle mast arm mounted signal heads for each
approach with in-road loop detectors. In addition, separate pedestal pole mounted pedestrian signal
heads with pushbuttons including ADA compliant curb ramps, are providedonall legsofthe intersection.
The traffic signal was determinedtooperateina fully actuated, two-phase manner with each roadway
being serviced under their own phase and concurrent pedestrian movements.

Middle Highway at Primrose Hill Road

The major intersection under reviewaspartofthis studyisthe junctionofthe Middle Highway with
Primrose Hill Road due its locationinrelationtothe site and the proposed access drivewaystothe
development. This intersection will servicealarge percentageofthe site related traffic duetoits locationinthe central portionofthe site, and direct linktohigher order roadwaystothe east. Primrose Hill Road
currently intersects Middle Highway, to essentially formanunsignalized 3-way, “T” type junction with Al/-
Way stop control. A fourth legofthe intersection that provides accesstothe subject property, historically
and physically exists, butisnot currently operationalasit has been gated foranumberofyearstorestrict
accesstothe site. All approachestothe intersection provideasingle all-purpose lane. There are stop

Proposed Residential Redevelopment Barrington, Rhode Island

signs/linesoneach approach andnomarked crosswalks are providedasthere arenosidewalks within the
intersection proper.

## 3.3 TRAFFIC DATA

Existing traffic flow characteristics for this area were developed fromatraffic counting program
conducted by Crossman and reviewofrecord data available from the RIDOT in the immediate project
area. The data collection effort included 5-day Automatic Traffic Recorder (ATR) counts on Middle
Highway and Primrose Hill Road, and peak hour (7:00-9:00 AM and 3:00-6:00 PM) Manual Turning
Movement Counts (TMC) at the Middle Highway intersections with Primrose Hill Road and County Road
during the weekday morning and afternoon peak periods in December 2023 and September 2024.

Based upon the ATR count program, Middle Highway north of County Road was foundtoservice
approximately 1,625 vehicles per day (vpd) onanaverage weekday and 1,450 vpdona Saturday. Ona
typical weekday, traffic volumes begintoincrease at 6:00 AM, withamorning peak hourofapproximately
155 vehicles occurring between 8:00 and 9:00 AM. After 9:00 AM, the volumes decreasedtobetween 75
and 130 vehicles per hour (vph), before increasingtothe afternoon peak after 2:00 PM. The afternoon
peak hour variedbyday but generally occurred between the hours of 3:00 and 5:00 PM with similar peak
volumesofapproximately 155 vph, whichiscoincident with the school dismissal periodsofthe
elementary and middle schools located along Middle Highway south of County Road.

Primrose Hill Road was foundtoserviceanaverageofapproximately 1,025 vehicles per day during the
week and approximately 910 vodona Saturday. Onatypical weekday along the Primrose Hill Road, traffic
volumes begintoincrease at 6:00 AM, withamorning peak hourofapproximately 80 vehicles occurring
between 8:00 and 9:00 AM. After 9:00 AM, the volumes slightly decreasedtobetween 60 and 80 vehicles
per hour, remaining relatively constant until after 6:00 PM when they decline for the remainderofthe
day. Daily afternoon peak hours varied occurring during the mid-day and early afternoon period, but
generally 80 vph are serviced during the peak hour.

In additiontothe ATR counts, Crossman completed manual turning movement countsatthe study
intersections noted along Middle Highway. Data was collected during the peak weekday periods from
7:00 to 9:00 AM and 3:00 to 6:00 PM when the proposed site and surrounding roadways would service
the highest combined peak volumeoftraffic. Figure 3 on the following page depicts the daily peak hour
turning movement volumes for the morning and afternoon peak periodsatthe study intersections. The
manual traffic count was foundtobe consistent with the ATR data for both roadways.

A review of RIDOT Seasonal Adjustment factors was also completedtodetermine average traffic
conditions along the study roadways. It was determined that the data obtainedona weekday during the
most recent count periods represent slightly higher than average traffic conditions along both roadways.
Basedonthese factors, count data was not adjusted lowertorepresent average traffic conditions for our
study, thereforeaconservative analysisoftraffic operations was completed. Complete count information
canbefoundinthe Appendix.

## 4.0 SAFETY ANALYSIS

In ordertodetermineifthere are any limiting factors affecting safety relatingtoaccesstothe proposed
residential project, the physical characteristics of Middle Highwayinthe project area were investigated.
These limiting factors would potentially include horizontalorvertical roadway geometric changesorroadside obstructions that limit sight distances for vehicles traveling along the roadorentering the road
fromaside streetordriveway location. In this instance, the Stopping Sight Distance requirementisa
design standard necessarytopermit turning vehiclestosafely enter and exit the developmentatthe
existing intersection with Primrose Hill Road, andatthe proposed site access road intersections with
Middle Highway. In additiontothe reviewofphysical roadway features, a reviewofrecord crash data
from the Barrington Police Department was also completedtodetermineifthere wasafrequency,
severity, or patternofcrashesinthe project area that requires mitigation.

## STOPPING SIGHT DISTANCE

Stopping Sight Distance (SSD) is the minimum distance thatadriver travelling alongaroadwayator near
the design speed, requiresinordertoadequately perceive, react and safely cometoa stop priortoreachinganobjectinits travel path and avoidacollision. The available and required SSD areafunctionofthe roadway geometry and design speed respectively, and are factoredinwhen determining the
appropriate and safe locationofa site drivewayorroadway intersection.

A reviewofthe horizontal and vertical curvature of Middle Highway along the property frontage
determined that the roadway canbedescribedasgenerally straight and level within the project area
extending north of County Road
through the Primrose Hill Road
controlled junction. These features
canbeseeninthe adjacent
photograph looking north along the
property frontage seenonthe left.

Based upon the roadway geometryasdescribed and the available sight
distances determined through field
analysisatthe study intersections, a
reviewofthe required stopping
sight distance was undertakentoensure safe operations. The
required SSD is based upon the speedoftraffic travelling along the roadway and this valueiscomparedtothe availableormeasured SSD to determineifthis safety measureissatisfied. In determining the
required SSD, the design speedofthe roadway mustbeestablished. The most recent editionofthe
American Association of State Highway and Transportation Official’s (AASHTO’s) publication A Policyoni=) CROSSMAN ENGINEERING 10
--- Page 96 ---

Proposed Residential Redevelopment Barrington, Rhode Island

Geometric Design of Highways and Streets, Table 3-1 is referencedindetermining the required stopping
sight distances, whichisbasedonthe design speeds for the roadway.

One methodofdetermining the design speedofa roadwayisreferencedinthe RIDOT Highway Design
Manual. On roadways withaposted speed limit less than 40 mph, the design speedisestimatedtobe
the posted speed limit, plus 5 mphinurban areas, and plus 10 mphinrural areas. To determineifthe
minimum requirements for safe SSDs were metinthis study, a design speed of 30 mph was utilized for
evaluating the stopping sight distance on Middle Highway.

In additiontoevaluating the SSD based upon the posted speed /imit and resultant design speed, tobeconservativeatthe Middle Highway site access road locations, actual speed data was obtainedtodetermine the 85"" percentile speed for drivers travelling along Middle Highwaytothe south of Primrose
Hill Road. The 85" percentile speed represents the speedatwhich 85 percentofdrivers are travellingator slower, andisutilized when availableinthe analysisofrequired sight distances. Basedonspeed data
obtainedaspartofthe data collection program, the 85"" percentile speedoftraffic travelling along Middle
Highway withaposted speed limit of 25 mph, was determinedtobe 33 miles per hour for northbound
and southbound vehicles. It shouldbenoted that travel speeds along the property frontage are highly
variable and relatively low duetothe Al/-Way stop-controlled intersection at Primrose Hill Road and the
traffic signal control at County Road and range between 25 and 35 mph.

Based upon the roadway geometryasdefined for Middle Highway, the sight distances availableatthe
existing intersection of Primrose Hill Road andatthe proposed site access road intersections were
determinedtobeinexcess of 500 feettothe north and southofeach location. These values are greater
than the required safe stopping sight distance (SSD) of 155 feet basedonthe posted speed of 25 mph,
and the 200 feet for the AASHTO requirements for design speed of 30 mph established per RIDOT policy,
and the 230 feet required for the actual travel speeds of 33 mph along the property frontage.

Also, as partofthe safety analysis, a reviewofcrash statistics within the study area was completed. Data
was obtained from the Town of Barrington Police Department foranindustry standard three-year period
(2018, 2019, and 2022) to determineifany locationinthe immediate vicinityofthe development
experiencedahigh frequencyorpatternofcrashes. Duetothe COVID-19 pandemic, the 2020 and 2021
data were not requestedastraffic patterns and volumes were notably impacted and not representativeofaverage traffic conditions. A total of 17 crashes occurredinthe project area over the three-year study
period, withnofatalities.

Summarizing the data, a majorityofcrashes (70%) within the study area occurredattwo locations
including the Middle Highway intersections with County Road and with Primrose Hill Road. At the
intersection with County Road where 10 crashes occurred, the most common collision types seen were
angle and rear-end collisions, which aretobe expectedatsignal-controlled junctions duetothe
numerous starting and stopping movements required for the change intervals, and non-protected left
turn movements. An averageofthree crashes per year indicatesarelatively low crash frequency foranintersection requiring signalization.

Se CROSSMAN ENGINEERING 11
--- Page 97 ---

Proposed Residential Redevelopment Barrington, Rhode Island

Only two crashes occurredatthe All -Way stop-controlled Primrose Hill Road intersection. They were
both minor property damage crashes causedbydriver errorintraversing the junction. The remaining
crashes were non-intersection along the segmentsofroad relatingtobacking vehicles, an impaired driverordistracted driver.

Based upon the historical accident data obtained from the local police, andareviewofexisting roadway
operations, geometry, physical features, and the proposed development plan, it does not appear that any
significant physical safety deficiencies presently exist along the servicing roadwaysorat the major
junctions requiring mitigation.

## 5.0 IMPACT ANALYSIS

## 5.1 TRIP GENERATION

To determine the traffic impactofa proposed development, estimatesofanticipated traffictobe
generatedbya particular land use mustbecalculated. As previously discussed, the proposed residential
project consistsofconstructing multiple residential buildingsofdiffering sizes and types from single family
homestoapartmentsina village style environment, resultingina total of 344 residential units.
Additionally, the west wingofthe stone manner containing approximately 20,000 square feetofavailable
building area, willberepurposed predominately for common space accommodating neighborhood
activities and management services for the village, withalimited 1,500 square foot commercial element
that wouldbefocusedona small neighborhood business use. Site amenities will also include open space
and walking trails for residents. Access and egresstothe site willbeprovidedbymodifying the existing
drivewayatits intersection with Primrose Hill Road and constructing two secondary intersections on
Middle Highway for efficient accesstothe neighborhood. Figure 4 on the following page depicts the
proposed redevelopment plan.

For this study, estimated traffic volumes for the residential redevelopment project were basedonthe useoftrip generation data obtained from the Trip Generation Manual, an informational report publishedbythe Institute of Transportation Engineers (ITE), a national professional organization for traffic and
transportation engineers. The data providedinthe ITE report are basedonextensive traffic studies for
numerous typesofland uses (residential, commercial, industrial, etc.). This data has been foundtobe
very reliable and providesasound basis for estimating future tripstonew development projects.

For the proposed 344 residential units, Land Use Code 210 Single Family Detached Housing (58 units),
Land Use Code 215 Single Family Attached Housing (66 units), Land Use Code 220 Multifamily Housing
(Low-Rise), (220 units) were reviewed for applicabilityindevelopinganestimateofsite related vehicles
trips. For the retail componentofthe project Land Use Code 822 Strip Retail Plaza (1,500 square feet)
was determinedtobe the most applicable. Table 2 providesasummaryofthe peak hour volumes
estimated for the proposed village type project utilizing the ITE factors. The appropriate worksheets from
the manual are includedinthe Appendix, along with the trip estimate calculations.

## 5.2 FUTURE TRAFFIC CONDITIONS

In ordertoproperly assess the traffic impacts fromadevelopment, future traffic conditionsofarea
roadways shouldbeestimated for the period when the developmentisconstructed and fully occupied.
Typically, the expansionofbase trafficiscalculated whenaprojectistobeconstructed overanextended
period, typically between threetofive years. For purposesofthe redevelopment project, traffic was
projected overafive-year horizon.

The Town of Barrington has seenanegligible population growth over the past two decades and has
declined for 16 of the last 20 years. This trendisprojectedtocontinue through 2040 by the Office of
Statewide Planningasthereislittle available land for further development. Tobeconservativeinour
analysis, a half percent per year growth rate was utilized for the study. The half percent rate was appliedtothe existing volumes and then the site-specific volumes estimated for the peak hours were then addedtoestablish the Future 2029 Build traffic condition.

In developing the intersection volumestobe analyzed under build conditions, Crossman reviewed the
proposed site layouttodefine potential vehicle circulation patterns within the village andtothe pointsofaccess/egress whereadirectional distributionofthe site traffic was estimated. The distribution was basedonjourneytowork data for the community and from the current traffic patterns defined from the traffic

Se CROSSMAN ENGINEERING 14
--- Page 100 ---

Proposed Residential Redevelopment Barrington, Rhode Island

counting program completed for this project. Based upon the locationofthe site and local infrastructuretomajor routes, itisassumed that 55/50 percentofthe site trips willbeoriented to/from the south and
15/25 percentofthe site trips willbeoriented to/from the north along Middle Highway and 30/25 percentofthe site trips willbeoriented to/from the east for the morning and afternoon periods respectively. The
volumes were then distributed throughout the network basedonexisting traffic patternsatthe major
junctions. Figure 5 on the following page depicts the estimated future traffic volumesatthe study
intersections.

## 5.3 OPERATIONAL ANALYSIS

The keytoany traffic impact analysisisthe evaluationofroadway operations during peak traffic periodsonthe servicing roadway system. This condition would occur when the site-generated traffic, combined
with the traffic volumesonthe main roadway, resultinthe highest one-hour volume serviced alongaroadway segment, or throughanintersection. Reviewofrecord traffic data found that the weekday
morning and afternoon peak hours would represent this worst-case combinationofsite-generated traffic
with the servicing roadway peak traffic period.

The Highway Capacity Manual methodology provides the most accurate meansofevaluating traffic
capacity and delays for roadways and intersections. The resultsofthis procedure are expressedinterms
of Level of Service (LOS). Level of Serviceisa qualitative measureoftraffic flow efficiency basedonanticipated vehicle delays. For example, LOS “A” represents the best condition with littleorno delay,
while LOS “F” indicates that the roadway/intersectionisat full capacity resultinginextended vehicle
delays and potential queuing. Table 2 outlines the Level of Service delay criteria presentedinthe Highway
Capacity Manual for signalized and unsignalized intersections.

TABLE 2 — Highway Capacity Criteria

Unsignalized Delay Signalized Delay
Level of Service Per Vehicle (sec) Per Vehicle (sec)
## A <10 <10
B >10 and <15 >10 and <20
C >15 and <25 >20 and <35
D >25 and <35 >35 and <55
E >35 and <50 >55 and <80
## F >50 >80

## 6.0 CONCLUSIONS AND RECOMMENDATIONS

In summary, the study has shown that the proposed residential redevelopment project access and
circulation plan has been designedtoprovidealeveloftraffic safety and efficiencyonthe servicing
roadway system. The safetyofthe study intersections of Middle Highway with Primrose Hill Road and the
Site Access Roads were reviewed for geometry and sight distances. The study intersections were
determinedtoprovide sufficient sight distancesinaccordance with AASHTO criteria for visibility and
decision makingofdrivers attemptingtoenter/exit main street traffic from the development. Analysisofthe crash data along the servicing roadway determined there wasnofrequencyorseverityofcrashes
within the study area requiring safety enhancements.

One element that shouldbeinvestigate further with developmentofthe subject propertyisthe
accommodations for pedestrian circulation within andtothe new village neighborhood. Based upon
reviewofthe preliminary site plans, sidewalks and walking trails are proposed within the new
neighborhood providing for safe and adequate pedestrian access through the development. The
connectiontothe local street sidewalk infrastructure though, requires further coordination with town
and state officialsasthere are missing segments along Middle Highwayaspreviously noted. New
sidewalks and crosswalks canbeinstalled strategically along the adjacent roads including improvements
proposedatthe Primrose Hill Road intersection requiredatthe existing driveway access. These
improvements can provide linkage and connectivityofpedestrian facilitiesinthe immediate vicinityofthe developmenttomaintainasafe pedestrian environment within and adjacenttothe Belton Court
Village project.

The resultsofthe operational analysis determined that the estimated minor increaseintraffic during the
daily peak periods resulting from the proposed residential project will haveaminor impactonoverall
traffic operations along the servicing roadways and intersections, particularly during the daily morning
and afternoon peak hours when the residential neighborhood would generate the highest daily traffic
volumes. Existing intersections will maintainagood levelofservice withaminor increaseindelays and
acceptable operations. The proposed site access road intersections are estimatedtooperateina safe
and efficient manner.

Therefore, based upon the data collectedonthe servicing roadways, the analysis completedaspartofthis study including recommendations, the proposed residential redevelopment project was determinedtohave adequate and safe accesstoa public street, and will not haveanadverse impactonpublic safety
and welfareinthe study area.

ITE Land Use Code 210 — Single-Family Detached Housing

September 2024 © CROSSMAN ENGINEERING
--- Page 173 ---

Land Use: 210
Single-Family Detached Housing

Description

A single-family detached housing site includes any single-family detached homeonan individual
lot. A typical site surveyedisa suburban subdivision.

Specialized Land Use

Data have been submitted for several single-family detached housing developments with homes that
are commonly referredtoas patio homes. A patio homeisa detached housing unit thatislocatedona small lot with little (or no) frontorback yard. In some subdivisions, communal maintenanceofoutside groundsisprovided for the patio homes. The three patio home sites total 299 dwelling
units with overall weighted average trip generation rates of 5.35 vehicle trips per dwelling unit for
weekday, 0.26 for the AM adjacent street peak hour, and 0.47 for the PM adjacent street peak hour.
These patio home rates basedona small sampleofsites are lower than those for single-family
detached housing (Land Use 210), lower than those for single-family attached housing (Land Use
251), and higher than those for senior adult housing — single-family (Land Use 251). Further analysisofthis housing type willbeconductedina future edition of Trip Generation Manual.

Additional Data

The technical appendices provide supporting informationontime-of-day distributions for this
land use. The appendices canbeaccessed through either the ITETripGen web apporthe trip

For 30 of the study sites, dataonthe numberofresidents and numberofhousehold vehicles are
available. The overall averages for the 30 sites are 3.6 residents per dwelling unit and 1.5 vehicles
per dwelling unit.

The sites were surveyedinthe 1980s, the 1990s, the 2000s, and the 2010s in Arizona, California,
Connecticut, Delaware, Illinois, Indiana, Kentucky, Maryland, Massachusetts, Minnesota, Montana,
New Jersey, North Carolina, Ohio, Ontario (CAN), Oregon, Pennsylvania, South Carolina, South
Dakota, Tennessee, Vermont, Virginia, and West Virginia.

Source Numbers

100, 105, 114, 126, 157, 167, 177, 197, 207, 211, 217, 267, 275, 293, 300, 319, 320, 356, 357, 367,
384, 387, 407, 435, 522, 550, 552, 579, 598, 601, 603, 614, 637, 711, 716, 720, 728, 735, 868, 869,
903, 925, 936, 1005, 1007, 1008, 1010, 1033, 1066, 1077,1078, 1079

--- Page 178 ---

Land Use: 215
Single-Family Attached Housing

Description

Single-family attached housing includes any single-family housing unit that sharesawall withanadjoining dwelling unit, whether the walls are for living space, a vehicle garage, or storage space.

Additional Data

The database for this land use includes duplexes (definedasa single structure with two distinct
dwelling units, typically joined side-by-side and each withatleast one outside entrance) and
townhouses/rowhouses (definedasa single structure with threeormore distinct dwelling units,
joined side-by-sideina row and each withanoutside entrance).

The technical appendices provide supporting informationontime-of-day distributions for this
land use. The appendices canbeaccessed through either the ITETripGen web apporthe trip
generation resource pageonthe ITE website (https://www.ite.org/technical-resources/topics/trip-

The sites were surveyedinthe 1980s, the 1990s, the 2000s, and the 2010s in British Columbia
(CAN), California, Georgia, Illinois, Maryland, Massachusetts, Minnesota, New Jersey, Ontario
(CAN), Oregon, Pennsylvania, South Dakota, Utah, Virginia, and Wisconsin.

Source Numbers

168, 204, 211, 237, 305, 306, 319, 321, 357, 390, 418, 525, 571, 583, 638, 735, 868, 869, 870, 896,

ITE Land Use Code 220 — Multifamily (Low-Rise)

September 2024 © CROSSMAN ENGINEERING
--- Page 183 ---

Land Use: 220
Multifamily Housing (Low-Rise)

Description

Low-rise multifamily housing includes apartments, townhouses, and condominiums located within
the same building withatleast three other dwelling units and that have twoorthree floors (levels).
Various configurations fit this description, including walkup apartment, mansion apartment, and
stacked townhouse.

+ Awalkup apartment typicallyistwoorthree floorsinheight with dwelling units that are accessedbya singleormultiple entrances with stairways and hallways.

«+ Amansion apartmentisa single structure that contains several apartments within what appearstobeasingle-family dwelling unit.

« A fourplexisa single two-story structure with two matching dwelling unitsonthe ground and
second floors. Accesstothe individual unitsistypically internaltothe structure and provided
throughacentral entry and stairway.

« Astacked townhouseisdesignedtomatch the external appearanceofa townhouse. But, unlikeatownhouse dwelling unit that only shares walls withanadjoining unit, the stacked townhouse
units share both floors and walls. Accesstothe individual unitsistypically internaltothe
structure and provided throughacentral entry and stairway.

Multifamily housing (mid-rise) (Land Use 221), multifamily housing (high-rise) (Land Use 222),
affordable housing (Land Use 223), and off-campus student apartment (low-rise) (Land Use 225)
are related land uses.

Land Use Subcategory

Data are presented for two subcategories for this land use: (1) not closetorail transit and (2)
closetorail transit. A siteisconsidered closetorail transitifthe walking distance between the
residential site entrance and the closest rail transit station entrance is % mileorless.

Additional Data

For the three sites for which both the numberofresidents and the numberofoccupied dwelling
units were available, there wereanaverage of 2.72 residents per occupied dwelling unit.

For the two sites for which the numbersofboth total dwelling units and occupied dwelling units
were available, an average of 96.2 percentofthe total dwelling units were occupied.

The technical appendices provide supporting informationontime-of-day distributions for this
land use. The appendices canbeaccessed through either the ITETripGen web apporthe trip

252 ~=‘Trip Generation Manual 11th Edition - Volume 3 ite=
--- Page 184 ---

For the three sites for which data were provided for both occupied dwelling units and residents,
there wasanaverage of 2.72 residents per occupied dwelling unit.

Itisexpected that the numberofbedrooms and numberofresidents are likely correlatedtothe
trips generatedbya residential site. To assistinfuture analysis, trip generation studiesofall
multifamily housing should attempttoobtain informationonoccupancy rate andonthe mixofresidential unit sizes (i.e., numberofunitsbynumberofbedroomsatthe site complex).

The sites were surveyedinthe 1980s, the 1990s, the 2000s, the 2010s, and the 2020s in British
Columbia (CAN), California, Delaware, Florida, Georgia, Illinois, Indiana, Maine, Maryland,
Massachusetts, Minnesota, New Jersey, Ontario (CAN), Oregon, Pennsylvania, South Carolina,
South Dakota, Tennessee, Texas, Utah, and Washington.

## © CROSSMAN ENGINEERING
--- Page 189 ---

Land Use: 822
Strip Retail Plaza (<40k)

Description

A strip retail plazaisan integrated groupofcommercial establishments thatisplanned,
developed, owned, and managedasa unit. Each study siteinthis land use has less than 40,000
square feetofgross leasable area (GLA). Becauseastrip retail plazaisopen-air, the GLA is the
sameasthe gross floor areaofthe building.

The 40,000 square feet GFA threshold between strip retail plaza and shopping plaza (Land Use
821) was selected basedonan examinationofthe overall shopping center/plaza database. No
shopping plaza withasupermarketasits anchorissmaller than 40,000 square feet GLA.

Shopping center (>150k) (Land use 820), shopping plaza (40-150k) (Land Use 821), and factory
outlet center (Land Use 823) are related uses.

Additional Data

The technical appendices provide supporting informationontime-of-day distributions for this
land use. The appendices canbeaccessed through either the ITETripGen web apporthe trip

California, Delaware, Florida, New Jersey, Ontario (CAN), South Dakota, Vermont, Washington, and
Wisconsin.

## 1.0 PROJECT NARRATIVE

1.1 Introduction

InSite Engineering Services (IES) has prepared this reportinconjunction with the accompanying
Comprehensive site plans “Belton Court” for SHINEHARMONY HOLDINGS, INC. The report summarizes
the proposed useofthe site and stormwater management system for the re-developmentofthe former
Zion Bible Institute site . The subject site (“Belton Court”) is geographically locatedinthe Primrose Hill
section of Barrington along the westerly side of Middle Highway opposite the intersection with Primrose
Hill Road.

The 36.975+/- acre ( 1,160,635 s.f.) Siteisidentifiedbythe Town of Barrington as:

e Assessor’s Plat 14, Lots 2, 4, 13, 341, 342 and 343

e addressed as 33 Middle Highway
The proposed redevelopmentisa Comprehensive Permit for 344 unitsofwhich eighty-six (86) units
willbeaffordable housing.

The proposed stormwater managementofthe site for “Belton Court” has been designedinaccordance
with the guidelinesofthe Town of Barrington Regulations and the Rhode Island Stormwater Design and
Installation Standard Manual unless otherwise noted. This reportistobeusedinconjunction with the
Site Plans “Belton Court”. The stormwater management plan has been developedtoemploy various
types of Best Management Practices (BMP’s) to control peak runoff rates, provide water quality,
promote groundwater recharge and provide sediment removal. Storm flows from the proposed
construction willbetreatedonsite and dischargestooffsite areas willbeequalorbelow preconstruction
rates. Itisanticipated that there willbeno flooding impactsornegative impactstothe downstream
outfalls resulting from this system.

## 2.0 EXISTING SITE CONDITIONS

2.1 Property Description

The siteisthe former campusofthe Zion Bible Institute and priortothat the Barrington College from
1950-1985. The structure along Middle Highwayisthe former manor house of Frederick S. Peck,
constructed in 1906. Zoning for the site is Senior Residential Village District. The abutting properties
along Middle Highway north of Primrose Hill Road and northofthe site are zoned “R 10” and are typically
residential single-family homes. Properties south of Primrose Hill Road and southofthe property are
zoned “R 25” residential single-family homes.

The property originally consistedofeight major buildings andafew small out- buildingsonthe site.
Besides the manor house, the other structures containedagymnasium, classrooms, dormitories and
maintenance facilities. Severalofthe buildings have been demolished over the past few yearsasnotedonthe existing conditions plan.

501 Great Road « Unit 104 = North Smithfield RI 02896 = T 401-762-2870 = F 401-762-2870 = www.insiteengineers.com
--- Page 231 ---

“Belton Court” Stormwater Management
Middle Highway Page 4 of 26
Barrington, R.I. July 11, 2025

Accessisprovidedtothe propertybya “main” paved driveway located off Middle Highwayatits
intersection with Primrose Hill Road. Additional accesstothe siteisprovidedbya drivewaytothe southofthe manor house.

The site has accesstoall utilities including public water, public sewer, gas electric and cable. The siteisserviced by BCWA connecting at Middle Highway for the main water line with hydrants locatedinthe
main driveway. Additional water services for “Belton Court” are also connected from Middle Highway.
Sewer serviceisprovided viaasewer main locatedinthe main driveway which connectstothe East
Providence sewer system via easements through private properties , Great Neck Road to County Road.
The siteisservicedbyoverhead electric and underground gas.

The existing site topographyismoderatelytogently sloping, ranginginelevation of 58 at Belton Court,
fallingtoa low pointofelevation 33 at the drainage swale located along the westerly property line. The
siteispredominantly improved with bituminous paved driveways, bituminous walkways and open areasoflawn. A wooded areaislocated southofthe “main” driveway bordering the wetland area.

Referring to RIDEM’s Environmental Resource Maps, the site does not lie within a Natural Heritage Area.
The site area does not fall within a FEMA designated Flood Hazard Zone.

Wetlands were delineated by Ecosystem Solutions, Inc., Warwick RI on November 17, 2021. The wetland
flags were located along the southwesterly and westerly property lines and are depictedonthe site
plans.

Existing stormwater managementofthe site comprisesofeither, directing runoff from impervious and
lawn areastocatch basins and manholes ultimately dischargingtoan existing drainage swale,
encompassedbyan easement, orbyoverland flowtothe wetland areas.

The existing site was analyzedasfive Subcatchment Areas with the design points (DP) being the
Bordering Vegetated Wetlandtothe South, The Bordering Vegetated Wetlandtothe West and towards
Middle Highway.

e Subcatchment Pre-A- Approximately 60% of the catchment areaisgrassed. The remaining areaiswooded and buildings with associated parking . The stormwater flows uncontrolled to Middle
Highway .

e Subcatchment Pre-E- Approximately 75% of the catchment areaisgrassed. The remaining areaiswooded and buildings with associated parking . The stormwater flows uncontrolled to Middle
Highway .

e Subcatchment Pre-D - Approximately 4% of the catchment areaisimpervious. The impervious
land useispaved parking and roads. The remaining areaiswooded and grass coverage . The
stormwater flows uncontrolledtothe Bordering Vegetated Wetland along the South property
line.

e Subcatchment Pre-B - Approximately 15% of the catchment areaisimpervious. The impervious
land useispaved parking and roads. The remaining areaiswooded with the majority being grass
coverage . The stormwater flows uncontrolledtothe Bordering Vegetated Wetland along the
West property line.

501 Great Road « Unit 104 = North Smithfield RI 02896 = T 401-762-2870 = F 401-762-2870 = www.insiteengineers.com

Middle Highway
Barrington, R.I. July 11, 2025

e Subcatchment Pre-C - Approximately 23% of the catchment areaisimpervious. The impervious
land useispaved parking and roads. The remaining areaiswooded with the majority being grass
coverage . The stormwater flows uncontrolledtothe Bordering Vegetated Wetland along the

West property line.

Pre-Development Rates of Runoff and total volumes are summarized in Tables 2 and 3.

## 3.0 - PROPOSED SITE CONDITIONS

3.1 Comprehensive Preliminary Site Plan

The “Belton Court” site plan proposes redevelopmentofthe existing property with improvements
describedasfollows.

e Demolitionofthe buildingsasnotedonthe site plan. The “carriage house and Belton
Tower aretoremain. The Carriage Houseistoberefurbished into Common space.

e Constructionofaccess roads off Middle Highwayatexisting entrance opposite
Primrose Hill Road and north and south of Primrose.

e Development of 344 units based in Multifamily buildings, six unit buildings, 2 over 2
townhouses, standard townhomes, Tower single family homes, Cottages and Single
Family homes

e Developmentofassociated parking lots , Access Driveways and garages.

e Constructionofstormwater management system utilizing LID strategiestoinclude the
following:

e Bioretention Basins, and Infiltration Basin
e Public sewer and water - install new sewer and water service
e Install Underground Electrical for electric and Data service.
e Install Gas service
e Landscaping
e Low Impact design of Stormwater management system

3.2 Stormwater Management Considerations

Consideration was giventoconserving environmentally sensitive features and minimizing impactonthe
existing hydrology.

The comprehensive drainage designisseparated into sixty Subcatchment areas. The total drainage area
analyzedisapproximately 36.5+/- Acres. Post Development Design Points are the same as Pre
Development . Design point D (DP D) being the Bordering Vegetated Wetlandtothe South, Design point

501 Great Road « Unit 104 = North Smithfield RI 02896 = T 401-762-2870 = F 401-762-2870 = www.insiteengineers.com
--- Page 236 ---

“Belton Court” Stormwater Management
Middle Highway Page 9 of 26
Barrington, R.I. July 11, 2025

B (DP B) is The Bordering Vegetated Wetlandtothe West and Design point A (DP A) is towards Middle
Highway.

Stormwater from the proposed Roads A, B, C, D, E and F is typically directedtoa paved waterway
discharging intoasediment forebay sizedtopretreat the stormwater. The stormwateristhen directedtoa bioretention basin where Recharge volume and Water Quality volume are provided. Overflow from
the Bioretention Basinsisroutedtoa subsurface systemofpipes and Drain manholes which outlettoeither an Infiltration Basin or Detention Pond. Catchbasins have been providedatthe street intersection
low points.

Proposed Alleys and parking lots aretobe Pervious Asphalt Paving.

For both the Pre-construction and Post Construction scenarios, the timesofconcentrations (TOC) for the
longest overland flow path within each area were determined using the TR-55 Method. Each flow pathisrepresented individuallyonthe subarea calculation sheets. A weighted curve number CN, was derived
for each subarea basedonproposed land usage and soil types. The respective pre-construction ratesofrunoff were interpretedasallowable release rates for post-construction design.

## 4.0 STORMWATER MANAGEMENT STANDARDS

This proposed Stormwater Management System complies with the current regulationsofthe Rhode
Island Department of Environmental Management (RIDEM). Compliance and applicabilityofthe eleven
(11) Stormwater Management Standards outlinedinthe Rhode Island Stormwater Design and
Installation Standards Manual, amended March 2015 are listed below.

4.1 Minimum Standard 1: LID Site Planning and Design Strategies

LID site planning and design strategies mustbeusedtothe maximum extent practicableinordertoreduce the generationofthe water runoff volume for both new and development projects.

The LID Site Planning and Design Criteria was utilizedinthe site planning process. The full listofapproved LID methods and/or procedures were exploredatthe site. LID methods proposed for
this site are bioretention systems and infiltration basin system, These items are documentedinthe Stormwater Management Checklist found in Appendix C.

4.2 Minimum Standard 2: Groundwater Recharge

Stormwater mustberecharged within the same sub watershedtomaintain baseflowatpre-
development recharge levelstothe maximum extent practicableinaccordance with the requirements
and exemptions described in Section 3.3.2 of the Rhode Island Stormwater Management Standards.
For development sites with 40% or more existing impervious coverage standard 2 mustbeaddressed.
Recharge and Water Quality shallbemanagedinaccordance with the acceptable techniqueofutilizing

on-site structural BMPstoprovide recharge and water quality management foratleast 50% of
development area.

The objectiveofthe groundwater recharge standardisto protect water table levels, stream baseflow,
wetlands, and soil moisture levels. Infiltrating stormwater may also provide significant water quality
benefits suchasreductionofbacteria, nutrients, and metals when infiltrated into the soil profile.

The Groundwater Recharge (Rev) volumeofstormwaterisbasedonthe amountofimpervious
area and hydrologic soil group (HSG) as follows:

Rev = (1”) (F) (i)/12
Where:

Rey = groundwater recharge volume (c.f.)

F = recharge factor
| = impervious area (s.f.)

The following chart shows the calculation and breakdownofthe Recharge Volume required for each
catchment and the Recharge Volume provided for that individual catchment.

RECHARGE - Re

Rev = 1"(F)(Impervious Area)1/12
F = 0.25 for HSG-C

4.3 Minimum Standard 3: Water Quality

For development sites with 40% or more existing impervious coverage standard 3 mustbeaddressed.
Recharge and Water Quality shallbemanagedinaccordance with the acceptable techniqueofutilizing
on-site structural BMPstoprovide recharge and water quality management foratleast 50% of
development area.

Stormwater runoff mustbetreated before discharge. The amount that mustbetreated from each
rainfall eventisknownasthe required water quality volume (WQy) andisthe portionofrunoff

containing the majorityofthe pollutants. The water quality volumeiscalculated using the following
equation:

WQ, = (1") Cimp area)(4/49)
A minimum (WQv) volume of 0.2 watershed inches (0.2” over entire disturbed area) is required.

The Total amountofimpervious area for the site is 10,391 c.f.
The minimum WAv for this area is
562091(.2”) (1/12) = 9368 c.f.

The total WQv provided for this site is 136,911 c.f. as tabulatedinthe attached chart.

The sizing for each bioretention area and sediment forebaytomeet ReV and WQvisincludedinthe
following section.

WATER QUALITY - WQv

WQv = 1"(Impervious Area)1/12

CATCHMENT IMPERVIOUS WQv WQv

4.4 Minimum Standard 4: Conveyance and Natural Channel Protection

Open drainage and pipe conveyance systems mustbedesignedtoprovide adequate passage for flows
leading to, from, and through stormwater management facilities foratleast the peak flow from the 10-
year, 24-hour Type III design storm event. Protection for natural channels downstream mustbesuppliedbyproviding 24-hour extended detentionofthe one-year, 24-hour Type II design storm event runoff
volume.

The pipe conveyance system has been designedtoprovide adequate passageofflows from the
10-year, 24-hour Type III design storm event.

4.5 Minimum Standard 5: Overbank Flood Protection

Larger storm events also can cause flood damage and other impacts. These impacts canbesignificantly
reducedbystoring and releasing stormwater runoffina gradual manner that ensures pre-development
peak discharges are not exceeded. Downstream overbank flood protection mustbeprovidedbyattenuating
the post-development peak discharge ratetothe pre-development levels for the 1-year, 10-year and 100-
year, 24-hour Type III design storm events.

The stormwater system was designed usingacomputerizationofthe Soil Conservation Service
(SCS) method, for the computationofrunoff hydrographs and peak discharge rates. All
stormwater management computations and pond modeling has been performed through the
useofthe Hydrocad V 10.0 Stormwater modeling software.

The rainfall duration intensity curves were developed from 24-hour rainfall intensities were
obtained from the U.S. Weather Bureau, for the Providence, RI area. The drainage analysisisbasedonthe SCS method witharainfall distribution Type III, for the 1-year, 10-year, and 100-year
design frequency storms.

1 year = 2.7 inches
10 year = 4.9 inches
25 year = 6.1 inches
100 year = 8.7 inches

Evaluationofthe contributing area(s), size, soil type(s), slope, and ground cover provide the
necessary information requiredtodevelop rainfall event hydrographs. Rainfall event
hydrographs are time/volume mathematical representationsofhow stormwater runoff volumeisgenerated from different size storm events overaperiod of 24 hours foraspecific watershed
area. Each hydrograph depictsabell-shaped curve where the area under the curve represents
the volumeofstormwater flowincubic feet.

Hydrographs were developed for each sub catchment area for existing-and proposed conditions.
The peak discharge rate was determined for each storm event. The site was designedtoreduce
the stormwater volume generated for each storm even including the 100-year design storm

4.6 Minimum Standard 6: Development and Infill Projects

The purposeofthis minimum standardisto establish the alternative requirements for projectsorportionsofa project where existing impervious areas willberedevelopedorwhere the site qualifiesasinfill. Innocaseona developmentorinfill project shall the levelsofstormwater treatment and
rechargebeless than the levels priortoinitiationofthe proposed project.

The Belton Court project does not meet the redevelopment standards.
Total Pre-Construction Impervious Area (TIA) (Existing conditions in 2025) = 189,928 S.F.
Site Area = 1,610,635 sf.
Wetland Area = 264,409 s.f.
Site Size(SS) =1,346,225 s.f.
TIA/SS = 189,928 sf/ 1,346,225 sf = 0.14
0.14< 0.4 therefore does not meet redevelopment
The project will meet all the requirements of Standard 6. Stormwater treatment and recharge
willbegreater than existing levels.

4.7 Minimum Standard 7: Pollution Prevention

All development sites require the useofsource control and pollution prevention measurestominimize
the impact that the land use may haveonstormwater runoff. These measures shallbeoutlinedina
stormwater pollution prevention plan. The intentofthis standardisto prevent, to the maximum extent
practicable, pollutants from coming into contact with stormwater runoff.

This Standardisaddressed in Minimum Standard 10 and 11 below.

4.8 Minimum Standard 8: Land Uses with Higher Potential Pollutant Loads

Stormwater discharges from land uses with higher potential pollutant loads (LUHPPLs) require the useofspecific source control and pollution prevention measures and the specific stormwater BMPs
approved for such use.

The Haley’s Commons siteisnotaland use with higher potential pollutant loads (LUHPPL).
Standard 8 is not applicable for this project.

4.9 Minimum Standard 9: Illicit Discharges

All illicit dischargestostormwater management systems are prohibited, including discharges from
OWTS, and sub-drains and French drains near OWTSs thatdonot meet the State’s OWTS Rules
(setbacks vary dependingonthe capacityofthe OWTS, the typeofconveyance system, and the
sensitivityofthe receiving waters). The stormwater management systemisthe system for conveying,
treating, and infiltrating stormwateronsite, including stormwater best management practices and
any pipes intendedtotransport stormwatertoground water, surface water, or municipal separate
storm sewer system (MS4). Illicit dischargestothe stormwater management system, i.e., illicit
connections, are discharges not entirely comprisedofstormwater that are not specifically authorizedbya National Pollutant Discharge Elimination System (NPDES) or RIPDES permit. The objectiveofthis
standardisto prevent pollutants from being discharged into MS4s and Watersofthe State, andtosafeguard the environment and public health, safety, and welfare.

The owner shallberesponsible for the prohibitionofillicit dischargesonsite. The site hasaconnectiontothe existing sewer system.

4.10 Minimum Standard 10: Construction Activity Soil Erosion, Runoff, Sedimentation, and
Pollution Prevention Control Measure Requirements

Erosion and sedimentation control (ESC) practices mustbeutilized during the construction phaseaswellasduring any land disturbing activities.

The site shallbedevelopedina mannertominimize land disturbancesinaccordance with State
of Rhode Island Soil Erosion and Sediment Control Manual issued 2009 and amended 2015. The
following specific construction strategies, techniques and erosion control measures are more
specifically describedasfollows:

## 1. Avoid and Protect Sensitive Areas and Natural Features

Areasofexisting and remaining vegetation and areas that aretobe protected during construction
are delineatedonthe plans. The proposed activities are consistent with Minimum Standard 1,
Low Impact Development (LID) Site Planning and Design Strategies and have been designedtomaximize the protectionofnatural drainage areas.

## 2. Minimize Area of Disturbance

Limits of Disturbance (LOD) are clearly markedonall SESC plans. The amountofland area for the
project site has been minimized. Existing vegetation will remaininplacetomaximum extent
possible. The project has been designedtocontrol the peak runoff and flows under the post
construction design. The site has been designedinaccordance with Minimum Standard 1, Low
Impact Development Site Planning and Design Strategies, Section 3.3.1.3., Appendix A Checklist
1.D. and Chapter Four — LID Site Planning and Design Strategies, Section 4.5.1.

## 3. Minimize the Disturbance of Steep Slopes

The site development will minimize the steep slopesonthe property. The maximum slopeonthe
property is 5 %. The site design will comply with Minimum Standard 1, Low Impact Development
Site Planning and Design Strategies, Section 3.3.1.3 and Appendix A Checklist 1.D. Locating Sites
in Less Sensitive Areas, and Chapter Four — LID Site Planning and Design Strategies, Section 4.5.1
Avoid the Impacts.

## 4. Preserve Topsoil

The site operator shall preserve and stockpile existing topsoil. The topsoil willbereusedonsite.
All remaining material willbehauled off site.

## 5. Stabilize Soils

The site shallbestabilized immediately whenever clearing, grading, excavating activities have
permanently ceased within the site. Hydro seed willbeused throughout the site. Any disturbed
soils exposed prior to October 15th willbeseededbythat date. Any areas thatdonot have

501 Great Road « Unit 104 = North Smithfield RI 02896 = T 401-762-2870 = F 401-762-2870 = www.insiteengineers.com

“Belton Court” Stormwater Management

Barrington, R.I. July 11, 2025

adequate vegetative stabilization by November 15th willbestabilized through the useofdegradable mulches that will cover and protect soil surfaces. If construction continues within the
site during the period from October 15th through April 15th, care willbetakentoensure that only
the area required for that day’s workisexposed, and all erodible soil will re-stabilize within 5
working days.

## 6. Protect Storm Drain Inlets

The site operator shall install inlet protection measuresatexisting catch basins and paved
waterways that remove sediment from discharge priortoentry into the storm drain system. The
operator will clean and/or remove and replace, the protection measuresassediment
accumulates. Accumulated sediment adjacenttothe inlet protection measures willberemovedbythe endofthe same work dayinwhichitis found.

## 7. Protect Storm Drain Outlets

Haybales and riprap aretobe usedatthe outfalltoprotect and prevent scour and erosionatdischarge points.

## 8. Establish Temporary Controls for the Protection of Post- Construction Stormwater Control
Measures

Straw wattles shallbeinstalledtoprotect bioretention basins and infiltration areasasthey are
installed and throughout the construction phaseofthe projectsothat they will function properly
when they are brought online. The erosion and sediment control plan identify areas where
infiltration measures are proposed and the site contractor shall restrict construction activity
within these areastoprevent compactionofthe area.

## 9. Establish Sediment Barriers

Straw wattles shallbeinstalled along the perimeter areasofthe site. The site contractor will
maintain the sediment barriersinaccordance with the maintenance requirements specifiedbythe product manufacturerorthe Rhode Island Soil Erosion and Sediment Control Handbook.

10. Divert or Manage Run-on from Up-gradient Areas

Straw wattles willbeusedtolimit stormwater flow from coming onto the project area, andtodivert and slow on-site stormwater flow from exposed soilstolimit erosion, runoff, and the
dischargeofpollutants from the site.

11. Properly Design Constructed Stormwater Conveyance Channels

The paved waterways and stone weirs have been sizedtohandle the peak flow from the 10-year,
24-hour Type Ill design storm.

12. Retain Sediment On-Site

The SESC Plan states different stormwater practices that control erosion, control run-off, and
control sediment. The combinationofpractices mustbedesignedtoprevent dischargesofsediment. The plans shall include inlet protection, construction entrances, and containmentofstockpiled materials.

13. Control Temporary Increases in Stormwater Velocity, Volume, and Peak Flows

The Construction Plan identifies the discharge points and locationofbioretention basins and
pervious pavers that contro! both peak flow rates and total runoff volume that will minimize
flooding, channel erosion, and stream bank erosion.

14. Construction Activity Pollution Prevention Control Measures

The SESC Plan describes allofthe pollution prevention measures that willbeimplementedtocontrol pollutantsinstormwater. The operator shall install, implement, and maintain effective
pollution prevention measurestominimize the dischargeofpollutantsinaccordance with the
SESC Plan requirements outlinedinthe Rhode Island Soil Erosion and Sediment Control Handbook

15. Control Measure Installation, Inspections, Maintenance, and Corrective Actions

The installationofstraw wattles willbecompletedbythe time each phaseofearth-disturbance
has begun. The site willbeinspectedbythe engineerorowneratleast once every seven (7)
calendar days and within twenty-four (24) hours after any storm event which generatesatleast
0.25 inchesofrainfall per twenty-four (24) hour period and/or afterasignificant amountofrunoff. Ifaninspection revealsaproblem, the operator will worktofix the problem immediately
after discovering the problem and complete such workbythe closeofthe next work day, if the
problem does not require significant repairorreplacement, orifthe problem canbecorrected
through routine maintenance. If new straw wattlesorhaybales are needed, the operator will
install and make operationalbyno later than seven (7) calendar days from the timeofdiscovery

Site specific areasofconcern are:

* Constructionofthe Bioretention and infiltration system
* Constructionofnew utilities

* Slope stabilization

* Preventionoferosion and sediments adjacent properties

* At the Engineer’s discretion, and following final gradingofthe surrounding slopes, a
lineofstaked straw wattle shallbeinstalled near the property linetoprevent eroded
soils from depositinginthe drainage systems andonadjacent properties, andalineofstraw wattle shallbeinstalledasshownonthe plans.

4.11 Minimum Standard 11: Stormwater Management System Operation and Maintenance

The stormwater management system, including all structural stormwater controls and conveyances,
must haveanoperation and maintenance plantoensure thatitcontinuestofunctionasdesigned.

The “Belton Court” owner, willberesponsible for the operation and maintenanceofthe stormwater
management system and allofits appurtenances. The owner will keepawritten recordofinspection
dates and findings, maintenance operations, and all repairs. Refertothe Stormwater Management
System Operation and Maintenance (Standard 11) sectionofthis report for the maintenance program.
A separate Operation and Maintenance Plan willberecorded and maintainedonsite.

O&M Access and Safety:

e Access to ALL Stormwater management systems shouldbesafe and efficient

e All egress and ingress routes shouldbemaintainedtodesign standard below:

e Access routes shouldbeinspected and maintained

e Obstacles preventing maintenance personnel and / or equipment access shouldberemoved

e Walkways shouldbeclearofobstructions and maintainedtodesign standards

e Roadways shouldbemaintainedtoaccommodate the size and weightofvehicles that use the
roadways

e Gravelorground cover shouldbeaddediferosion occurs (for example, asaresultofvehicleorpedestrian traffic)

e All fences shouldbemaintainedtopreserve their functionality and appearance

e Collapsed fences shouldberestoredtoan upright position

e Jagged edges and damaged _ fences should be _- repaired or _ replaced

5.0 Conclusions

The proposed “Belton Court” project will incorporateadrainage system comprising the use of BMP’s suchassediment forebays, bioretention basins, and infiltration ponds. The useofthese BMP’s result in:

An equalorless peak discharge rate for the 1-year, 10-year,25 and 100-year design frequency
storms.

Compliance with the Rhode Island Stormwater Design and Installation Standards Manual

80% TSS Removal

No detrimental effectsofthe water quality duetothe basin’s abilitytoretain and/or remove
nutrientsoractasa natural pollution filter.

No decreaseinflood storage capacity that would impair the wetland’s abilitytoprotect life and/or
property.

Minimal impactstothe natural characteristicsofthe wetland andtothe plants and habitatsofthe wetlands.

The drainage system has been designedtoaccommodate the anticipated flowsinaccordance
with Barrington’s Rules and Regulations. Pre- and Post-Construction Watershed Plans are
included, demonstrating watershed areas, flow paths and Curve numbers.

The discharge has been designedtowithhold the peak ratesofrunofftobelow the pre-
construction rate analyzed for the site. The system was designedtoprovide cleansingofthe
stormwater andtoprovide the required storage volumeinordertodetain the peak flow rates and
provideazero-net-increasetothe surrounding areas. The discharge with appropriate BMP’s,
accounts foracompositeofthe post construction controlled and uncontrolled flowsasindicatedonthe schematic diagram and watershed maps.

Itisthe opinionofthis engineer that the proposed “Belton Court” stormwater management
programisin compliance with the latest Rhode Island Stormwater Design and Installation
Standards Manual.

## BRISTOL COUNTY WATER AUTHORITY
July 31, 2025

Suanne Santos

InSite Engineering Services, LLC
1539 Fall River Ave, Suite 1
Seekonk, MA 02771

RE: Water Main Extension- Comprehensive Permit "Belton Court Village"-33 Middle Highway

Barrington

Dear Ms. Santos,

Thank you for the opportunitytoreview the preliminary design documents relatingtothe
proposed development at 33 Middle Highway in Barrington, Rhode Island (also referredtoas

“Belton Court Village”).

Bristol County Water Authority (BCWA) met with representatives of InSite Engineering
Services, LLC (InSite), Shine Harmony Holdings (Owner/SHH), Pare Corporation (Pare), and
the Town of Barrington on July 10, 2025. The intentofthe meeting wastodiscuss the water
availability request for Belton Court Village, the acceptanceofwhich being necessary for
preliminary approval of Belton Court Villagebythe Town of Barrington. BCWA has not yet
approved water availability for Belton Court Village duetothe contents of Pare’s hydraulic
analysisofthe development, dated April 7, 2025, and the lackofsupplemental information from
InSite/SHH requested by BCWA. In the summaryofthe analysis, Pare states the following:

“Itisalso estimated that the system can provideanavailable fire flowofapproximately
1,000 gpmtothe proposed development without causing the pressureinother partsofthe
systemtofall below 20 psi. SHH’ fire protection engineer should review this
informationtodetermineifthe estimated available fire flows are sufficienttomeet their

fire protection needs.”

Asofthe dateofwriting this letter, BC WA has not received any verification that 1,000 gpmissufficienttoprovide fire protection service to Belton Court Village. As discussedinthe meeting,
in ordertofully approve the project, the owner must fully demonstrate that the available supply
from the water system can support the project without detrimentally impacting the surrounding
customersorthe performanceofthe water systemasa whole.

However, the report does demonstrate that waterisavailable for Belton Court Village for
domestic use alone. As reported by Pare, supplyisavailableatanticipated pressures ranging
--- Page 434 ---

between 48 psi to 54 psi for domestic supply alone. No information was provided for irrigation
supply and its associated water supply demands.

It shouldbenoted that the hydraulic analysis performed by Pare representsavery unique
operational scenario/configurationofthe BCWA water system. This scenarioisbased upon the
current BCWA system configuration and maybesubjecttochange. If for some reason the
system configuration changes, e.g., the Fountain Ave. Tankisput back into service, thenafull
analysisofthe project’s water system impact for both domestic and fire flow demands willberequired.

Moving forward, BCWA will require all associated water supply demands for the projectinordertofully evaluate the project’s impactonthe water system. BCWA understands that this

information willbeprovidedasthe project’s design advances andwelook forwardtocontinuing
conversations with all parties involved. BCWA trusts that this letter satisfies the request from the

Town and SHH during July 10" meeting.

Please adviseifyou would liketomeettodiscuss this matter further. Andasalways, please feel
freetocontactmedirectly with any questionsorconcerns.

Thank Ng
Colin O° Hara
Project Manager

CC: Stephen Coutu, P.E., BCWA
Michael Crawford, P.E., BCWA
Dave Dasilva, BCWA
Charles Kalocsay, Shine Harmony Holdings
Herbert Durfee II], Town of Barrington

## STATEMENT AS TO CHANGES MADE FROM PRE-APPLICATION STAGE;
## REQUIRED ZONING APPROVALS; USE ALLOCATIONS BY SQUARE FOOTAGE
## OF EACH PROPOSED USE; REQUESTED WAIVERS/AND ADJUSTMENTS;
## CONSTRUCTION SCHEDULE; IMPROVEMENT GUARANTEES; CONSISTENCY
## WITH AFFORDABLE HOUSING NEEDS AND COMPREHENSIVE PLAN

A, Changes Made Since Pre-Application

Since the pre-application meeting with the Planning Board on March 5, 2004, there have
not been substantive changes. 344 Units were contemplatedatthat time.

B. Required Zoning Approvals

None. This applicationisproceedingasone for a Comprehensive Permit under the Low
and Moderate Income Housing Act, RI Gen. Laws § 45-53-1, et seg., whereby the Planning

Board actsasthe singular permitting authority.

C. Use Allocations By Square Footage

The projectisa residential project entirely. All dwelling units and common community

spaces. The preserved portion of Belton Court, a wing comprising approximately 5,000 S.F.,
would, ifitis the preferenceofthe Board, be utilized foracommunity coffee shop and/or

mailing and office supportorsimilar general community convenience facility.

D. Requested Waivers And Modifications/Adjustments

See Tab 4 narrative, particularly page 6 thereto and Attachment | hereto

E. Construction Schedule; Improvement Guarantees

The project will commence upon procurementofrequired local and state permits andbeconstructed overaperiod of 6 - 10 years. The internal infrastructure willbeprivate and not

involving improvement guarantees associated with public improvements.

## MEMORANDUM

To: Barrington Town Officials

From: Shineharmony Holdings, Inc.

Date: August 6th, 2025

Re: Application fee for Preliminary Site Plan Approval

Shineharmony Holdings, Inc. (“Shineharmony” ) appreciatestothe opportunitytosubmit
its application for preliminary site plan approval relatedtothe developmentofthe former

Zion Bible College campus in Barrington, RI.

In connection with the application, Shineharmonyisrequesting that the Town of Barrington
consider waiving the $34,400 application fee (344 units * $100 per unit) basedonthe
following factors:

e Since acquiring the property in 2011, Shineharmony has paid over $1.5 millioninproperty taxestothe town of Barrington without burdening the town’s expendituresoneducation and municipal services.

e The proposed development of 86 affordable units will benefit the townbyreducing
its deficiencyofaffordable housing. Basedonthe high current levelofconstruction
costs and interest rates, the financial burdenondevelopers seekingtodeliver such

unitsisvery significant.

We thank you for your considerationofthis request and believe that the factors above
provide ample support for granting such waiver. We look forwardtoyour prompt response.
In the meantime, pleasedonot hesitatetocontact Charlie Kalocsay at 443-994-1522 oratcharles.kalocsay@gmail.com with any questionsorconcerns.

Shineharmony Holdings, Inc.

Charl. Krhttirimy.

Charles Kalocsay —- President
