Located approximately 6 miles from downtown Providence, at roughly 37 acres, this siteislocated in Barrington Rl.
Itisboundedbyexisting residential developmentonall 4 sides, with direct access to Middle Highwayonthe east
edgeofthe site. The siteishome to Belton Court, a 55,000 square foot mansion built from 1905 to 1928 that was
addedtothe National Register of Historic Places in 1976.

Regulation Review
2015 Comp Plan Summary

A preliminary summaryofthe 2015 Comprehensive Plan and existing zoning requirements
are includedonthe following pages. Someofthe comprehensive plan density bonuses are
achievable, and most requirements are feasible.

Base Density: 6-8 du/acofdevelopable land
Density Bonus:

e Preservation and restoration of Belton Court and grounds associated with its historic use,
including the central courtyard and front lawn between the building and Middle Highway.

e Preservationofthe mature vegetationinfront of Belton Court along Middle Highway
Density bonus: include the Belton Court Historic Register District acreageinthe develop-
able land area.

e Provision either on-siteoroff-siteofpublic open space and recreation of 500 square feet
per unit, or 200 square feet more per unit required per Developer Guidance “5H”. Density
bonus: include land utilized for commercial use, including associated parking, in the
developable land area.

Open Space Required:
e 300 sq.ft. / unit (excluding wetlands)

Streetscape / Parking:

e Sidewalk min 1 side @ 5’

e Sidewalksonboth sides for MF & MU

e Required new/improved sidewalks along Middle Hwy

e Street trees shallbeplantedatregular intervals, consistent with the Town’s Land
Development and Subdivision Regulations, to define the street edge, buffer pedestrians
from vehicles, and provide shade. Trees shallbelocatedina planting strip between the
curb and sidewalk.

e Utilities shallbeunderground
e Pedestrian scale lighting required
e On-street parking not within 30’ of corner.

e At the Zion Bible Institute site, Belton Court shallberestoredina manner thatisconsis-
tent with the Secretaryofthe Interior’s Standards for the Treatment of Historic Properties.
The historic grounds associated with Belton Court shallbepreserved (the area, including
the building and grounds, totals approximately 4.5 acres).

e« Tobedesignated Mixed-Use Village, a mixofusesisrequired.

Site Planning:

e Green Building principles required
e Stormwater shall follow LID

e Native vegetation shallberetained

## HOA:
e Requiredtomaintain common areas

Summarized here are excerpts from ACC
Market Study, dated June 2021.

Regional Market and Population:

e Market area covers East Providence down
o Newport, including Swansea, Westport,
Barrington, Bristol, Rumford, and Warren.

e Barringtonisperceivedasa desirable
ocation with the ‘Bestinthe State’ school
system and higher median household
income than less proximal communities.

e Population growth has beenona decline
regionally and locally andisforecastedoremain slow through 2026. Population
growth in Barringtonissignificantly less
han the region (.09% vsupto .20%)

« Median household income ($90.7k) is
well above national average ($64.7k) and
greater than regional ($72.4k in 1-5mi
proximity, and $59.4k in 5-10mi proximity).

e Unemploymentislower in Barrington at
5.6% vs. up to 8.9% regionally.

e There are anumberofmajor employersinthe region, primarily locatedincentral
and surrounding Providence area. Key
employers are healthcare and social assis-
tance. There are 49 employees per 100
residentsinthe MSA.

e Migration trends suggestaninfluxofout-of-state residents from larger metro
areas.

e Regional transportationisavailable, notinclose proximitytothe site.

e Housing Trends:

e« Outofstate renters and buyers areonan
uptrend duetomigration into the market
area.

e While single family housingisat high
demand, the median sales prices have
also beenonthe rise. Demand in
Barrington for sales and rentalsishigher
than supply.

e Majorityofcurrent developmentisin
suburban areas, primarilytothe westofthe bay.

Charrette Design Process

Over the courseofa 5-day charrette, held in Barrington from December

5 to 19, DPZ workedonsite with 6 designers and architectstodesign the
Belton Court Master Plan. During the week DPZ held 5 topic-focused meet-
ings with key stakeholders. These topic meetings were smaller-group meet-
ings with in-depth presentations and discussionsonspecific elementsofthe
master planning process. Topics ranged from zoning and entitlementtoinfra-
structure, environmental, and site programming, including the needsofthe
neighborhood.

Presentationtocity staff Site tour

Community meeting and presentation

Initial reactionstothe plansbythe charrette attendees were positive. Generally, they liked the following elements
that are visibleineach plan:

The possibilityofmulti-generational living;

The mixofbuilding typesonthe site;

The varietyofopen spaces and their possible uses;
The inclusionofneighborhood serving retailonsite;

The retention / celebrationofsomeofthe historic Belton Court buildings;

The adaptive reuseofthe rec centerasa community-wide amenity; and

The innovation designofthe village;

School capacity concerns were expressed, withanunderstanding that this may impact the total numberofapproved
units for the site.

Circulation

A collective systemofsidewalks, paths and trails will provide
pedestrians and bicyclists with more choiceofroutes, creatingaconnected, and diverse circulation ways that can tie into the
greater circulation network.

Suchaproposed network that promotes walking and cycling will
further contributetoa more sustainable community andahealth-
ier populace.

Streets are designedaspublic spaces. As such they playamuch
larger roleinthe public lifeofits users than just thoroughfares
for traffic. Since streets and open spaces constitute about 40%
of the total land area, they shouldbedesignedtobe contex-
tual, comfortable, and connected. Additionally they shouldbedesigned for safety where people walking, parking, shopping,
bicycling, working, and driving can cross paths safely.

Architecture Character

Principles of Architecture

As partofthe master plan design, the team studied different scalesofbuilding types and architectural
character, that would compliment the historic structures of Belton Court. Belowisthe listofprinciples
that set the direction for the proposed building types which followsonpages 62 to 69.

## 1. Regionally Influenced Architecture...An
Authentic Architecture. Well proportioned
simple forms with vertical openings, and
simple roof lines.

## 2. Mixofhousing types within each block...
building typesonblocks, to attract
multi-generational buyers, andtohelp createamore resilient environment.

## 3. Small is Beautiful - a mixofuses should
include small cottages, senior homes and
accessory dwelling units. Be contextualbykeeping higher density towards the centerofthe site and lower density adjacenttoexist-
ing SF neighborhoods.

## 4. Architecture that provides giftstothe
streettopromote walkability - Provide
courts, gardens, patios, stoops and porches.
Hide the car.

## 5. Celebrate the site’s history - Useofstone along the baseofbuildings where
appropriate.

Design Guidelines
--- Page 523 ---

Definitions

The following pages contain detailed site regulations
for Belton Court site. The terms defined here include
elements that are regulatedbythe urban and architec-
tural controls establishedbythe Design Guidelines.

Regulating Plan

A map precisely locating the various zoning categories.
The Regulating Plan also shows the form and locationofthe following plan elementsaslisted below.

Frontages

All frontages that are alongastreetoropen space
are Primary frontages, unless otherwise notedinthe
Regulating Plan.

Primary Frontage [A-Street]: A pedestrian-friendly street
with active usesatgrade, heldtothe highest standardofurban performance. Entrancestoparking garages
and service bays are prohibited, unless specifiedonthe regulating plan. Curb cuts for alley access maybepermitted, accordingtothe Regulating Plan. An A-Street
network formsacontinuous loop through the thorough-
fare network.

Retail Frontage

Some Frontages require further specification. The
Regulating Plan prescribes where retailisrequired and
whereitis allowed but not required.

Setbacks

All Frontages are assigned Setbacks. Setbacks specify
the build-to lineofthe building facade.

Building Height

Building Heightisassigned by Building Type and speci-
fiedonthe Regulating Plan.

Towers

Towers, permittedtoexceed maximum building height
are locatedonthe Regulating Plan and further regulatedinthe Design Standards and Guidelines.

72

Vista Termination

Locations whereastreet aims prominentlyata build-

ing facade — called a Vista Termination, are identified.
Buildings located at Vista Terminations shall respond
withabuilding elementofappropriate size and impacttoterminate the vistaina meaningful manner.

Open Space

Locations where specific open space types have been
calibrated, are identified.

Civic Building
Locations for Civic Structures are suggestedinthe

Regulating Plan and are further restrictedinsize accord-
ingtothe Open Space Types.

Curb Cuts

Curb cuts are indicatedonthe Regulating Plan, and are
permittedtobe within a 50 foot range from whereitis
indicatedinthe Regulating Plan.

Thoroughfares

Thoroughfare assemblies are assignedinthe Regulating
Plan.

Design Guidelines

## 1. Purpose

1.a. The purposeofthese Design Standards and Guidelines are to:

e Createapredictable framework for development thatisin keeping with the Belton Court
Master Plan.
e Coordinate the placement, orientation and designofbuildingstoensureacoherent, walkable

streetscape withinanew urban neighborhood.

e Encourage pedestrian-friendly development withavarietyofbuilding types that contributetoa robust economic base and support diverse locally oriented businesses and community
amenities andisin keeping with the Town’s Comprehensive Plan.

## 2. Applicability

2.a. In additiontozoning requirements, these Design Standards and Guidelines shall further guide the
developmentofthe Belton Court site.

2.0. Where conflicts arise, the most restrictive standard shall prevail.

Design Guidelines

## 6. Parking

6.a. On-street Parking for private streets

e On-street parking shall count towards minimum parking requirements accordingtothe
following:

- When on-street parkingisstriped, each striped space located directly along the prop-
erty shall count towards the minimum parking requirementofthat lot.

- When on-street parkingisnot striped, parkingiscalculatedatone space for every 25
feet located directly along the property and shall count towards the minimum parking
requirementofthat lot.

6.6. Off-street Parking

° Placement

- Surface parking lots are prohibited. Exceptions include:

fo) Parking for short term leases while permanent parking facilities are being
constructed, uptoa maximum of 12 months.

fo) A maximum 1 row deep head-in parking withasingle drive aisle maybeprovided within 100 feet of Middle Hwy.

fo) If the parkingisfully screenbyhabitable using along all sides.

- Structured parkingisprohibited. Exceptions include:

fo) The parking shallbelinedbyhabitable usesamin 24 feet deep.

fo) If not lined, the parking maybeslightly elevated, atamaximum 8 feet. The
exterior wall shallbedetailedasa building face, in keeping with the adjoining
building.

e Design

- Lots used for off-street parking shall meet the minimum frontage requirements and
shallbesetbackaminimum 10 feet from the property line.

- Any parking structures shallbewholly screenedbya buildingorliner building.
- Underground parking, if wholly underground, maybeplacedupto the property line.

- Walls screening parkingatgrade shall have small windows that alignorotherwise
coordinate with windowsinupper stories, creating the perceptionofan inhabited
ground floor.

e Access shallbeaccordingtothe regulating plan and limitedasfollows:

- Curb cuts and parking access shallbeaccordingtothe regulating planoraccessed
from rear alleys, where available.

- Parking access lanes shallbea maximum of 10 feetinwidth per lane.

e Blocks may exceed the maximum lengthsolongasa pedestrian passageisprovidedata
minimum 250 foot interval.

e Any portionofthe lot, in the Center Zone, facingapublic frontage, thatisnot occupiedbya
buildingordriveway shallbeoccupiedbya streetscreen.

e Frontage Yard Types shallbeassigned accordingtothe regulating plan andasdefined by

8.d. Facade Types
e Facade Types shallbeassigned accordingtothe regulating plan andasdefined by Table XX.

for each lot frontage.
8.e. Fences & Walls shallbeaccordingtothe following:

## 9. Building Massing & Articulation
9.a. Pedestrian Access

82

frontage.

Design Guidelines

- Design
fo) Office and retail shall have doors which are principallyofa translucent
material.
O Residential entries shallbedetailedasprivate and shallbeprincipally solid

with minimal glazing. Exceptions include entriestolobby’s which are staffed
between the hours of 7am and 9pm..

- Townhouses shall haveafront door that receives eitherastooporfront porch with
stairs that reach downtosidewalk level.

- Townhouse end units may locate their main entry eitheratthe frontorthe side.

9.b. Height & Articulation

Heightisfurther restrictedbybuilding type, andismeasuredinstories.

A maximum 50% of the rooforattic space maybehabitable and shall not count towards the
overall height.

Windows shallbeconsistent across facades, establishingasymmetrical and/or balanced
rhythm.

Where provided, facade breaks shouldbebrokenupin suchaway that each building facade
hasaclear and balanced composition that can readasa stand-alone building. Changesinmaterial, color, vertical and horizontal articulation, shall notbearbitrary. Changesinmate-
rialorcolor should correspondtovariationsinbuilding mass, and should comply with the
following:

- Be convincing. The goalisto createasenseofauthentic developmentofdistinct
buildings designedbydifferent architects.

- Make “buildings” that are themselves simple. Each building fagade shouldbemadeofa limited amountofmaterials, fouratmost.

- Demise lines shouldbeaccompaniedbya changeinbuilding setback only when
doingsomakes senseasan urban design (space-making) strategy. Otherwise,
unnecessarily wiggling undermines the street space.

- Each “building” fagade should look properifisolatedasa standalone structure, with-
out neighbors. This means that the fagadeisa clear, balanced composition withaproper center and edges.

Bay windows areanacceptable formofbuilding articulationifthe structureofbay windowiscarriedtothe groundorsupportedbybrackets proportionedtovisually carry the load above
it.

Townhouses shall have consistent materials and openings acrossacomplete row.

Each individual townhouse shallbeseparated from its neighborbya downspout.

9.c. Setbacks shallbeaccordingtothe regulating plan and “Table 1 Form Standards”.

9.d. Encroachments

Generaltoall:
- Balconies are encouraged and shall establishasymmetrical rhythm.

- Galleries are encouraged and may encroach into the setback according to Table 1. (tobecoordinated with zoning)

° Windows shall notbeextruded and shall haveatraditional profile.

° Brick mouldisrequired when windows are alongamasonry facade. The application and
profile shall follow American Vignola.

## RE: WETLAND DELINEATION
21 Middle Highway; A.P. 14, Lots 2, 4, 13, 341, 342, & 343
Barrington, Rhode Island

Ms. Qi,

Ecosystem Solutions, Inc. (“ESI”) has completedasite investigation for wetlands and wetland
jurisdictional areasonandinthe close vicinityofthe above referenced Property (“Property”). The
site investigation was conductedbya Professional Wetland Scientist on November 17, 2021. The site
investigation for wetlands was performedinaccordance with the Rhode Island Fresh Water
Wetlands Act (R.1I-G.L. 2-1-18 et. seq.) (“Act”), and the Rules and Regulations Governing the
Administration and Enforcementofthe Freshwater Wetlands Act, (RICR 250-15-5-3) (“Rules”). This
report details the differenceinclassification and protectionofthe wetlands on Propertyasof July 1,
2022

The subject Propertyislocated within the jurisdictionofthe RI Department of Environmental
Management (RIDEM). Any workoropinions stated by ESI are subjecttothe reviewofthe RIDEM.
Wetland delineation data forms are provided with this report and document vegetation, soils and
hydrology found on-site.

Property Description

The Propertyislocatedonthe west side of Middle Highway, across the street from its intersection
with Primrose Hill Road. Itisa site with substantial historical and recent disturbances. There are
many buildings on-site, the oldestofwhich beingalarge building referredtoas “Belton Court.” The
rest are buildings associated with ‘Barrington College” which moved onto the site around 1950.
Accordingtoour aerial photo interpretation, the buildings and fill that was deposited for the
buildings appeared on-site between 1951 and 1972. This means that all fill on-siteishistoric (pre-
Act and Rules) and therefore non-jurisdictional. The Propertyisborderedtothe south, and west by
Red maple swamp. Great Roadtothe south, Beverly Roadtothe west, Beaver Roadtothe north, and
Middle Highwaytothe east are all residential roads with densely established single family homes
near the Property.

Wetland Permitting Definitions
State-RIDEM & CRMC
--- Page 545 ---

As mentioned above, work and activitiesinand around wetland resource areas (freshwater wetlands
and their buffers and buffer zones) are regulated by RIDEM at this site. RIDEM regulates most work
and activitiesinupland-adjacent areas, and are definedasfollows:

e Jurisdictional Area: Includes any Freshwater Wetlands, Buffers, Floodplains, Areas subject to
Storm Flowage, Areas Subject to Flooding, andavariable width contiguous area extending
outward from a River, Stream, Drinking Water Supply Reservoir, or any other Freshwater
Wetland Type. The Jurisdictional Area includes all lands and waters that are subjecttoregulation under the Rules.

e Buffer Zone: A varying width area contiguous to, and extending outward from a Freshwater
Wetlandinwhich workisregulatedbythe RIDEM or CRMC (RIDEM for this Property). Any
work proposed within the Buffer Zoneofa Freshwater Wetland may require the creationofadditional Buffer (see “Buffer” definition below), if the lotisdeveloped (i.e. an areaofland
that consistsofbuildings, impervious surfaces, bare gravel, lawn, or landscaped areas). While
RIDEM has influenceonwork inside of Jurisdictional Areas, mostoftheir rules and
regulations are implementedonwork within the Buffer Zone. All “Buffer” (as explained
below) exists strictly within the Buffer Zoneofthe particular Freshwater Wetland.

e Buffer: An areaofundeveloped vegetated land adjacent to (within the Buffer Zone of) a
Freshwater Wetland thatistoberetainedinits natural undisturbed conditionoristobe
createdtoresembleanaturally occurring vegetated area. All work proposed within the Bufferofa Freshwater Wetland must meet the performance standards set forthinthe Rules through
avariance. This includes, butisnot limitedtomeeting the impact avoidance and minimization
requirementsaswellasmitigating for any work within the Buffer Zone. In section 3.7.1(B)(4)
of the Rules, thereisno standardinthe Rules for “minimum target for creationofnew Buffer”
for work within the Buffer Zoneona developed single-family house lot. Therefore, avoidanceofworkinthe Buffer Zoneisrequired when possible for the Property, howeverifwork within
the Buffer Zoneisunavoidable thereisno designated “minimum target for creationofnew
Buffer” to mitigate for said work. Thisisnotaguarantee that the RIDEM will not request
creation of Buffer.

e Setback: The minimum distance from the edgeofa freshwater wetlandatwhichanapproved
activityoralteration may take place. The Setback standards for primary structures (e.g.
residential dwellings, commercial. industrial structures, schools, churches, etc.) is that they
mustbelocatedtomeetasetback distanceofno less than the Buffer width plus twenty feet
(20’). Accessory structures mustbelocatedtomeetasetback distanceofno less than the
Buffer plus five feet (5’). Setbacks for Onsite Wastewater Treatment Systems (OWTS) are
such that the leach field and the required ten foot (10’) cleared zone arounditshallbelocated
outside the Buffer.

W21-1583 Ecosystem Solutions, Inc.
21 Middle Hwy.

Barrington, Rhode Island Page 2
--- Page 546 ---

Regulated Wetland Areas and Jurisdiction

Wetlands occurinthe western and southern portionsofthe site (This siteislocated within ‘River
Protection Region 2’):

Deciduous Swamp: The B-Series and D-series wetlands are best classifiedasa deciduous
swamp greater than one (1) acre but less than ten (10) acres. This swampisdominatedbyred maple (Acer rubrum), red oak (Quercus rubra), highbush blueberry (Vaccinium
corymbosum), sweet pepperbush (Clethra alnifolia), and cinnamon fern (Osmunda
cinnamomea) with silt loam soilsonvery low slopes.

Deciduous Swamp: The A, E, and F-series wetlands are best classifiedasdeciduous swamp
less than one (1) acresinsize. As with the B and D-series wetlands, these wetlands are also
dominatedbyred maple (Acer rubrum), red oak (Quercus rubra), highbush blueberry
(Vaccinium corymbosum), sweet pepperbush (Clethra alnifolia), and cinnamon fern (Osmunda
cinnamomea) with silt loam soilsonvery low slopes.

Property Specifications
Wetland area classification and jurisdictionatthis location under the new Rulesisexplained below:

Band D-Series

Wetland Permitting Jurisdiction

As mentioned above, work and activitiesinand around wetland resource areas (wetlands, ponds,
streams, and their buffer zones) are regulated by RIDEM at this site. If additional impervious surfaces
are addedtothe site that draintoareas within the Buffer Zones, evenifthey are outsideofthose
areas, require permitting. RIDEM regulates most work and activitiesinupland-adjacent areas, and
are definedasfollows:

e Performance Standards: The buffer withinabuffer zone shall consistofundeveloped
vegetated land. Whenaprojectisproposed withinapre-disturbed buffer zone, new buffer
mayberequiredtobe created withinaportionofthe buffer zonetoresembleanatural area.
At this site, we expect the performance standards for worktoadheretothe following:

o For proposed projectsoractivitiesona Property thatisgreater thanorequaltothree
(3) acres, thatisnotasingle-family residential lotofrecord, that contains one (1) or
more existing structures, and where the proposed land disturbance totalisgreater
thanorequal to 10,000 square feet, the minimum target for total buffer width is 25
feetinthe non-urban River Protection Region 2.

o Primary structures canbebuiltnocloser than 20 feet, from the outer Buffer boundary
accessory structures canbebuiltnocloser than 5 feet from the outer Buffer boundary,

and septic systems (including the 10’ cleared area surrounding) mustbebuilt outsideofthe buffer.

Applicants may request variancestothe performance standardsifthey cannotbemet. This means
that any work proposed within undeveloped, vegetated Buffer Zone, within 25’ of the wetland
boundary within disturbed Buffer Zone, or cannot meet the 20’ construction setback from a Buffer
will likely requireavariance under Rules §3.7.

## TECHNICAL MEMORANDUM

DATE: _ February 14, 2025 (DRAFT)
Revised: March 4, 2025 (DRAFT)
Revised: May 6, 2025

TO: Charles Kalocsay — President
ShineHarmony Holdings, Inc.

FROM: _ Brian Wrigley, P.E. — Managing Engineer
Brandon Blanchard, P.E. — Vice President

RE: Proposed Belton Court Village
Downstream Municipal Sanitary Sewer Capacity Analysis
Barrington, RI
Pare Project No. 24258.00

Pare Corporation (Pare) was tasked with completingasewer capacity analysisofthe existing
municipal sanitary sewer collection system located immediately downstreamofthe former Zion
Bible College campusinthe Town of Barrington, RI (the Town). ShineHarmony Holdings, Inc.
(SHH) is proposinganew residential developmentatthe property with 344 residential units. The
proposed development sitsonan approximate 37-acre plotofland andisbeing referredtoas “Belton
Court Village”.

Pare’s analysis incorporated:

e metered sewer flows within the Town’s collection system;

eanestimated amountofinfiltration and inflow (I/D) that may notbereflectedinthe metered
flows, basedonseasonal conditions during the monitoring period; andeprojected sanitary flow from the planned development.

Note that this analysis did not account for additional buildoutorother future developmentinthe
Town’s collection system.

EST Associates, Inc. (flow metering contractor contracted by SHH) initially performed flow
metering over a 4-week period from December 17, 2024 to January 16, 2025. Duetoinaccuracies
with someofthe data collected during that time, EST performedanadditional four weeksofflow
metering, from February 14, 2025 through March 14, 2025. Previous draft versionsofthis
memorandum presented preliminary findings, basedonthe initial roundofflow monitoring data.
This May 2025 updatetothe memorandum incorporates the most recent roundofflow metering
data.

Following review of Pare’s Draft Technical Memorandum (revision date March 4, 2025), the Town
expressed their concerns with potential impacts from sanitary flows from the proposed developmentonthe existing Bay Spring Pump Station (locatedatthe corner of Lake Avenue and Read Avenue).

The Town’s specific concerns were presentedinan email to SHH from Barrington Department of
Public Works (DPW) Director Alan Corvi on March 11, 2025, and then discussed further duringavirtual meeting held on March 20, 2025, between SHH, Pare and Town officials. Subsequently, Pare
was tasked with reviewing the existing pumping conditionsatthe Bay Spring Pump Stationtoevaluate and assess the station’s abilitytomanage the projected sanitary flow from the proposed
development.

Work Completed

Pare has completed the following tasks supporting the analysis and the developmentofthis technical
memorandum:

e Requested and reviewed existing Town of Barrington sewer records (GIS Mapping, Record
Drawings, Bay Spring Pump Station information, historical Town accounts and trends
including capacity and maintenance issues, rehabilitation and repairs, etc.).

Spring Pump Stationtothe south (locatedatthe corner of Lake Ave and Read Ave). This
flow path considersaproposed sanitary wastewater discharge from the planned
developmenttoan existing 8-inch diameter gravity sewer pipeonthe Town’s sewer
collection system in Great Road.

and review and analysisofdata collected during the following monitoring periods:

o Initial 4-week field data collection from December 17, 2024 to January 16, 2025; and
o Additional 4-week field data collection from February 14, 2025 to March 14, 2025.

monitoring efforts performed by EST:
o EST’s Flow Monitoring Reports and data received by Pare on January 30, 2025 and
April 2, 2025.

(InSite), the engineerofrecord for the planned development.

e Review and onsite evaluation (performed on April 21, 2025) of the existing pumping
conditionsofthe Bay Spring Pumping Station. Assistance provided by Pare’s subcontractor,
Harbor Controls, Inc., and the Town of Barrington Sewer Department.

Existing Conditions

Existing Sewer Infrastructure
The documented (Town of Barrington records) existing sewer pipe sizes between the development
and the Bay Spring Pump Station areasfollows:

e 8-inch diameter in Great Road and County Road (RI Route 103);
e 15-inch diameter along Richmond Avenue and Rogers Avenue; and
e 18-inch diameter, beginning through off-road easements that follow Annawamscutt Brook

across Haines Park Road from northtosouth, first through Haines Memorial State Park, and
continuing towards the Bay Spring Pump Station along various Town roadways.

The Existing Sewer Capacity Analysis Figure (Figure 1) provided as Attachment 1 depicts the
existing sewer alignment evaluatedaspartofthis study. Attachment 2 includesatabular summaryofeach pipe segment over this alignment. We are not awareofa unique manhole identification
system usedbythe Town, so Pare has added arbitrary manhole identification labels along the sewer
alignment for the purposesofthis evaluation. Figure 1 also shows the locationofflow meters and
the rain gauge installedatthe Barrington Department of Public Works for the flow metering effort.

The existing structures locatedonthe subject property are currently servicedbya vitrified clay
sewer that connectstothe City of East Providence’s sanitary sewer collection system via County
Road/Willett Avenue (Route 103). Pareisnot awareofan existing connection from the propertytothe Town of Barrington’s sewer system. Available records indicate that the existing clay sewer
ranges from 8-inch diameter leaving the property (throughaneasement to Great Road) to 12-inch
diameterasit crosses into East Providence from Barrington. This pipe and the City of East
Providence’s collection system has not been evaluated by Pare. Its approximate location and
alignment are also depicted on Figure 1, for reference purposes only.

Existing Sanitary Sewer Flows

Following reviewofthe flow meter data collected and provided by EST Associates, Pare
incorporated the recorded average flows and maximum, or peak, flows into the existing sewer pipe
capacity analysis for evaluation. The maximum (peak) flows that were usedinthe analysis represent
the highest recorded flow rateatthe metered locations during the monitoring period. The analysisofexisting average and peak flows within the pipe network indicates that this portionofthe Town’s
collection systemissufficiently sized for managing current sanitary flowinthe service area. In fact,
allofthe pipes appeartobe operating well below (30% or less) their full carrying capacity under the
peak flow conditions observed during the monitoring period. Thisisreflectedinthe “Existing Flow”
sectionofthe table in Attachment 2.

Bay Spring Pumping Station — Existing Pump Operation/Conditions

The Bay Spring Pumping Station, locatedonthe corner of Lake Avenue and Read Avenue, was
originally designed and constructedinthe late 1970’s/early 1980’s andisof “dry-pit/wet-pit”
construction. The station was most recently upgraded in 2006, replacing the existing pumps while
also addingathird pumptothe station with new piping, valves, and controls, amongst other
upgradestostation equipment and appurtenances. There are currently three (3) dry-pit submersible
--- Page 567 ---

am PARE

## CORPORATION

Mr. Charles Kalocsay - President (4) February 14, 2025 (DRAFT)
ShineHarmony Holdings, Inc. Revised: March 4, 2025 (DRAFT)
Revised: May 6, 2025

style solids handling pumps (manufactured by Morris Pumps/Yeomans Chicago Corporation), each
rated for pumping approximately 1,200 gallons per minute (gpm) at 130 feetoftotal dynamic head
(TDH) with nameplate data indicating that they were manufactured in October 2006.

The pumps’ motors are 75-hp and 3-Ph/460V power requirements. The pumps currently operateina
lead/lag/standby configuration with one pump operatingata time under normal flow conditions. The
pumps’ speeds are controlledbyvariable frequency drives (VFDs) and pump station instrumentationiscontrolled and managedbya programmable logic controller (PLC) locatedinthe station’s
supervisory control and data acquisition (SCADA) panelinthe control roomofthe station. Thereisan existing flow meter installed in-lineonthe pump station’s force main, located within the pump
roomofthe station. Liquid levels within the station’s wetwells are primarily measured and
monitored utilizingacompressed air “bubbler” level monitoring system. Pump operating rangeiscontrolledbythis system. Thereisa secondary, standby ultrasonic level monitoring system installedatthe station whichisavailable for use upon failureofthe primary bubbler level monitoring system.

During our onsite evaluation on April 21, 2025, we observed that each pump’s speedismanually settorunata maximum of 37 Hz, whichisequivalenttoapproximately 62% of the full speedofeach
pump basedona 60 Hz frequency. During our onsite pump evaluation, this pump speed producedapumping flow rate ranging between 550 and 600 gpm, as recordedbythe station’s flow meter.
Discharge head pressure was observedtobe approximately 50 to 55 feet (22 - 24 psi) under these
operating conditions, accordingtothe pressure gauges installedatthe station. Accordingtothe
Town’s Sewer Superintendent, the pumps have been operatingatthis speed and these settings since
the 2006 upgradeatthe station, and pump settings are typically not adjusted (manuallyorautomatically) for varying flow conditions suchaselevated influent flows during and following wet
weather.

Attachment 3 includesafield summary memorandumofour April 21, 2025 onsite evaluationofthe
pump station, including more detailed informationonexisting conditions and the resultsoffield
pump tests performed during our site visit.

Pare obtained the following information for the proposed residential development from InSite.

Sanitary wastewater flow from the proposed development was estimated basedonthe numberofhousing units, and more specifically the numberofbedrooms, provided to Pare along withareviewofavailable and widely accepted references and guidance documents. These include:

## 1. Rhode Island Department of Environmental Management (RIDEM) Onsite Wastewater
Treatment Systems (OWTS) Regulations [250-RICR-150-10-6 / Section 6.22B: Wastewater
Design Flows]

## 2. Rhode Island Department of Environmental Management (RIDEM) Office of Water
Resources (OWR) Flow Estimation Policy for Design of Sanitary Sewers

## 3. Technical Report #16 (TR-16), “Guides for the Design of Wastewater Treatment Works’,
2011 Edition (as Revised in 2016), preparedbythe New England Interstate Water Pollution
Control Commission (NEIWPCC).

Since the planned residential units will vary widely between one (1) and four (4) — bedroom units,
Pare selected RIDEM’s OWTS Regulations for the estimationofsanitary flows from the
development. This was deemed most appropriate, because other references assign sanitary flow
estimates basedonthe numberofhousing units, independentofthe numberofbedrooms provided.
Accordingly, Pare’s calculationsofthe projected sanitary flows from the residential units are
provided below.

The development will also includeanapproximate 5,000 square-foot common areaorcommercial
space. Actual useofthis spaceisyettobe determined, but potential uses being considered includeadeliormarket. The OWTS Regulations provide estimated wastewater flow from these typesofuses
--- Page 569 ---

am PARE

## CORPORATION

Mr. Charles Kalocsay - President (6) February 14, 2025 (DRAFT)
ShineHarmony Holdings, Inc. Revised: March 4, 2025 (DRAFT)
Revised: May 6, 2025

as 350 gpd for facilities under 5,000 square feet and 700 gpd for facilities 5,000 square feetorlarger.
Because this representsasmall and insignificant fractionofthe total estimated flow from the
development’s residential uses, it was not addedinour estimateofpeak flow described below. Any
changeinthe planned useofthis space that increases sanitary flow above the estimates usedinthis
analysis shouldbeevaluated further.

Max Daily Flow Calculation:

The RIDEM OWTS Regulations estimate 115 gallons per day (gpd) per bedroom for sanitary flow,
representing a ‘Maximum Daily Flow’ per bedroom. Using thisasa benchmark estimate for flow
per bedroom, and referencing the 759 total bedrooms proposed for the “Belton Court Village” from
information provided to Pare, the following calculations were usedtodetermine the estimated Max
Daily Flow:

Max Daily Flow = #of Bedrooms x Max Flow per Bedroom

Max Daily Flow = 759 Bedrooms x 115 gpd per Bedroom = 87,285 gpd

Peak Flow (Design Flow) Calculation:

In accordance with standard sanitary sewer design guidance (TR-16), sanitary sewers shouldbedesignedona peak hourly design flow basis. The peak hourly flow rateisdefinedasthe largest
volumeofflowtobe received duringaone-hour period.

Using TR-16 Figure 2-1: “Ratio of Extreme Flow to Average Daily Flow’, the following ratios were
interpolated from the figuretodetermine the Peaking Factor for the estimated sanitary flow under
conditions where project average daily flows are under 100,000 gpd:

1.0 — Average Daily Flow Ratio

3.0 —Maximum 24-hr Ratio to Avg

5.5 — Peak on Maximum Day Ratio to Avg

1.83 — Ratio of Peak Flow to Maximum 24-hr Flow (Peaking Factor for this application)

Peak Hourly Flow = Maximum Daily Flow x Peaking Factor = Design Flow

Peak Hourly Flow (Design Flow) = 87,285 gpd x 1.83 = 160,000 gpd

A peak sanitary flow of 160,000 gpd from the proposed development was used for this analysis.
Downstream Sewer Capacity Analysis

Gravity Sewer Collection System

Pare reviewed the Town’s sanitary sewer records and populated the summary table provided as
Attachment 2 with documented existing sewer pipeline information, where available (location, pipe
length, diameter, material, upstream and downstream manhole inverts, pipe slope, etc.) for the direct
sanitary sewer flow path between the anticipated connection from the subject property in Great Road
and the Town’s Bay Spring Pumping Station. Using Manning’s Formula/Equation, Pare then

calculated the individual pipe segment capacities for this alignment. Attachment 2 contains the

Pare utilized maximum recorded flows from the flow monitoring data provided by EST Associates
and treated those flow valuesaspeak hourly sanitary flows. The maximum recorded system flows
were combined with the estimated peak flow from the proposed developmenttoanalyze the future
flow conditions within each segmentofsewer over this sanitary flow path. Duetoinaccurate meter
records obtained from Flow Meter Locations #2 and #3 during the monitoring period, flow measuredatthe most downstream meter near the Bay Spring Pump Station (i.e., Flow Meter #1) was evenly
distributedtoeach upstream segmentofpipeupto the location of Flow Meter #4, installed in
Richmond Avenue, for the pipe capacity analysis describedinthis technical memorandum.

An allowance for infiltration was also addedtothe existing flow monitoring data. At the depthsofthe existing Town sewer, itisassumed that flow monitoring data that was collected during the
monitoring period will containaportionofflow that canbeattributedtogroundwater infiltration.
However, basedoninput from Barrington DPW personnel, local groundwater table elevations are
historically low and current infiltration rates maybeconsidered lower than normalasa result.
Accordingly, Pare has includedanallowance for additional groundwater infiltration within the
collection system basedonrecommendations from TR-16, which provide foranallowance of 250-
500 gpd per inch-diameter-mile [Ref. Section 2.2.3.3]. Tobeconservative, Pare used 500 gpd per
--- Page 571 ---

am PARE

## CORPORATION

Mr. Charles Kalocsay - President (8) February 14, 2025 (DRAFT)
ShineHarmony Holdings, Inc. Revised: March 4, 2025 (DRAFT)
Revised: May 6, 2025

inch-diameter-mile for this analysis. Attachment 2 containsacolumn labeled ‘Cumulative
Infiltration Allowance’ with unitsofgallons per day. This additional infiltration has been
incorporated into the pipe capacity analysis.

The estimated total flowineach segmentofsewer, under proposed conditions, is reflectedbythe
column entitled “Combined Future Peak Flow w/Infiltration (MGD)” in Attachment 2. This
represents the sumofmetered existing peak flow, allowance for additional infiltration, and future
peak hourly flow from Belton Court Village. A column has been includedinthe tabletoidentify the
projected operating capacitiesofthe existing sewer pipes displayedasa percentageofthe theoretical
full pipe capacities.

Overall, the majorityofthe Town’s sewer collection system downstreamofthe proposed Belton
Court Village development appearstohave available capacitytomanage the future sanitary flows
projectedbythis analysis. However, three (3) sections of 8-inch diameter pipe along County Road
are projectedtooperate over 55% or moreoftheir existing carrying capacity (percentages displayedinbold, red font) in the proposed condition. The flattestofthese pipe segmentsisestimatedtobe
operatingatover 85% of its capacity with the projected peak flow from the development. Twoofthe
three subject pipe segments are documentedtohave slopes well below the TR-16 recommended
minimum slope (0.0040 ft/ft) for an 8-inch diameter sewer.

The pipe capacity analysis indicates that all but oneofthe remaining Town sewer pipes are projectedtooperate below 35% of their existing carrying capacities under future peak flow conditions, anddonot appeartopresent capacity concerns. It shallbenoted that after the Town’s sewer increases from
8-inch diameter to 15-inch diameter from County Road to Richmond Avenue, available pipe
capacity increases substantially.

Bay Spring Pumping Station

Following reviewofthe existing pumping conditionsatthe Bay Spring Pumping Station, it appears
that there maybeadditional capacity availabletomanage the projected peak sanitary flow from the
proposed development, pending modificationstothe current pump speed control parameters and/or
current wetwell operating levels. The projected peak design flow from the proposed developmentisapproximately 160,000 gpd, whichisequivalenttoa rate of 111 gpm. The data collected during
Pare’s pump evaluation on April 21, 2025 indicates thatanincreaseinpump speed would
conversely increase the pumping flow rate from the station. During our onsite testing, an increaseinpump speed from the existing speed setting at 37 Hz [62% of the rated 60 Hz frequency] to 45 Hz
[75% of the rated 60 Hz frequency] resultedina net increase single pump outputofapproximately
400 gpm, far exceeding the projected peak flow from the proposed development.

Modificationstothe pump station maybepossibletooffset the increaseinflow from the proposed
development, such as:

e Increase pump operating speeds, as noted above;

e Increase wetwell operation level (to reduce the frequencyofpump starts/stopsasneeded);
and
--- Page 572 ---

ra PARE

## CORPORATION

Mr. Charles Kalocsay - President (9) February 14, 2025 (DRAFT)
ShineHarmony Holdings, Inc. Revised: March 4, 2025 (DRAFT)
Revised: May 6, 2025

e Utilize the existing Automatic Wetwell Level Control programming logic thatiscurrently
notinuse, which would use influent ratestothe stationtoadjust pump speed and discharge
rate accordingly.

Conclusions and Recommendations

Basedonour analysis, the majorityofthe collection system between the Belton Court Village site
and the Bay Spring Pumping Station appearstohave sufficient capacity for the flow anticipated
from the planned development. However, three sections of 8-inch pipe in County Road would
exceed 55% or moreoftheir carrying capacity with the added flow from the development. The
Town has already expressed concern with the resulting conditiononthis partofthe system, and
ShineHarmony Holdings shouldbepreparedtoaddress this concern. Pipe slope willbegovernedbythe existing manhole invert elevations located upstream and downstreamofany proposed sewer pipe
replacement, so installing new 8-inch pipeatincreased slopeisnot feasible. Rather, increasing
carrying capacitybyreplacing these sectionsofpipe with larger pipe (i.e., 12-inch or 15-inch
diameter) is the more likely alternative. Also, constructingapump station from the developmenttothe Town sewerisnot recommended, duetothe anticipated design and construction costsaswellasthe long-term operation and maintenance requirementsapump station will present.

The projected flow from the developmentisprojectedtoincrease flowtothe Town’s Bay Spring
Pumping Stationonthe order of 15% under average daily conditions, basedonan average dry
weather flow of 210,000 gpd providedbythe Town and flow meter data collectedaspartofthis
evaluation. Because this representsasignificant increaseinflowtothe pump station, ShineHarmony
Holdings shouldbepreparedtowork with the Towntoidentify which modificationstopump
operations are necessarytoaccommodate this increaseinflow. Basedonour onsite evaluation, it
appears that the pumping capacity from the station canbeincreased, but the final operating
condition will needtobe optimized with input from the Town.

Itisalso understood that Town of Barrington officials inquired whether SHH performedasimilar
evaluationofthe existing connection from the site, formerly usedbythe Zion Bible College, to the
City of East Providence sanitary sewer collection system via County Road/Willett Avenue (Route
103). As noted above, Pare has not performed this evaluation andweunderstand connectingtothe
Town of Barrington collection system was explored first since all other site permittingisbeing
pursued with the Town. While system upgrades and modificationstopump operations, suchasthose
noted above, will increase the system’s carrying capacityinresponsetoadded flow the
development, SHH should explore the viabilityofconnectingtothe City of East Providence systemona parallel track.

Pareisavailabletomeet and discuss our findings with you and the Townatyour convenience.

## IELD RE ORT MEMORANDUM

DATE: May 5, 2025

TO: File

CC: Brandon Blanchard, P.E. — Vice President
FROM: _ Brian Wrigley, P.E. — Managing Engineer

RE: Bay Spring Pump Station — Existing Pumping Conditions Evaluation
Proposed Belton Court Village
Barrington, RI
Pare Project No. 24258.00

This memorandum summarizesanonsite evaluationofpumping operationsatthe Bay Spring
Pumping Station in Barrington, RI. Pare Corporation (Pare) performed the evaluation on April 21,
2025 to understand existing pump operating conditions relativetothe firm pumping capacityofthe
station. Harbor Controls, a subconsultant to Pare and the Town’s pump instrumentation and controls
consultant, assisted Pare during this evaluation.

istairat ad_eerai dii s

The Bay Spring Pumping Station, locatedonthe corner of Lake Avenue and Read Avenue, was
originally designed and constructedinthe late 1970’s/early 1980’s andisof “dry-pit/wet-pit”
construction. A single 18-inch ductile iron gravity pipe influenttothe station entersaconcrete
influent channel within the “wet-pit” sideofthe station. Upon entering the primary influent channel,
flow passes throughacomminutor (cutting device for reducing debris sizeinthe waste stream) and
splits into eitheroftwo individual concrete wetwell sections viaasecondary concrete influent
channel that runs perpendiculartothe primary influent channel. Thereisa standby bypass influent
channel aligned paralleltothe primary influent channel that containsamanually cleaned bar rack
(for isolation and bypassofthe comminutor and primary channel). The individual wetwell sumps are
situated below the primary and secondary influent channels and are isolated from one another. They
areofsquare configuration with concrete infill chamfersonall four sides, sloped towardsacentral
sump where the pumps’ suction piping intakeislocated. Influent flow canbedirectedtoeither oneorboth wetwell sectionsbyoperatingaseriesofisolation sluice gates installed within the secondary

Town’s Sewer Superintendent informed Pare that keeping one wetwell section online and one
offlineistypical operating conditionsatthis station (for the purposeofinspection and maintenance).

pumps (as manufactured by Morris Pumps/Yeomans Chicago Corporation) locatedatthe pumping

Pare observed that each pump’s speedismanually settorunata maximum of 37 Hz, whichisequivalenttoapproximately 62% of the full speedofthe pump (100% speed = 60 Hz). At the time of
Pare’s onsite pump evaluation, this pump speed producedapumping flow rate ranging between 550
and 600 gpm, as recordedbythe station’s flow meter. Discharge head pressure was observedtobe
approximately 50-55 feet (22-24 psi) under these operating conditions accordingtothe pressure
gaugesinthe station. Accordingtothe Town’s Sewer Superintendent, the pumps have been

typically not adjusted (manuallyorautomatically) for varying flow conditions suchaselevated

wetwell withaminimum pump speed setting of 30 Hz (50% speed) andamaximum pump speed
setting of 60 Hz (100% speed). Wetwell liquid level range settings are currently settoa minimum

is riaaieratarai

A 24-hr historical flow chart provided to Pare by Harbor Controls indicates that the pumps currently
cycle on/off approximately five times per hour under normal flow conditions. The Town’s Sewer
Superintendent stated that the designated “lead pump” is alternatedona weekly basis through
manual operator selection (Pump #1, #2 or #3) on the SCADA panel.

Peak hourly flows, pumping flow rates, and historical pump operational trends were not initially
provided to Parebythe Town, but anecdotal information from the Town indicates that one pump
operates under typical dry-weather conditions and two pumps may occasionally operate during
periodsofhigh flow conditions. As reportedbythe Barrington DPW and Sewer Department, the
Bay Spring Pumping Station currently manages approximately 210,000 gallons per day (gpd), or
about 1.5 million gallons (MG) each week, of sanitary sewer flow under normal, dry-weather
conditions. The station reportedly manages approximately 280,000 to 300,000 gpd, or about 2.0 to
2.1 MG per week, during and immediately following wet-weather events and resultant high flow
conditions. This equatestoan approximate 33% to 40% increaseinflowtothe pumping station
during wet weather conditions.

Accordingtothe Town, the pumping station has had difficulty managing influent flows during
extreme wet weather events, butnoreported evidenceofsewer system overflowsorprivate property
backups were presented to Pare. The Town also suspects that thereisa potential downstream
hydraulic restrictionatthe Town’s force main connection into the City of East Providence’s
collection system, specifically observed during wet weather (high flow) conditions. Pareisaware
that the force main discharge from the Bay Spring Pump Station directly connects into another,
larger force main pipe (viaawye connection along the East Bay Bike Path) that conveys flow fromatleast one other pump station within the Town (Brickyard Pump Sation) priortodischarge to East
Providence. The competing force main flowislikely leadingtothis hydraulic restriction experienced
during wet weather flow conditions. The Town reportsareduced pump output from the Bay Spring
Pump Station under these conditions.

radesierraeaa

During the onsite pump evaluation performed on April 21*, Pare and Harbor Controls conducted
multiple pump drawdown teststogather existing pump performance data for analysis. All three
pumpsatthe station were individually observed operatingattheir existing speed settings (62%
speed), each operatedatboth 100% speed and 75% speed, and then twoofthe three pumps were
operatedatthe same time at 100% speedtoobtain the firm pumping capacityofthe pumping station
(Pump Test No. 4). Harbor Controls’ Field Report has been provided in Attachment 1.

NOTE: Existing wetwell operating levels were maintained for the pump testing and were not
adjusted. Pump speeds were manually adjusted by Harbor Controls during the testing, but the
pumps were allowedtorun automatically basedonthe PLC’s programming for wetwell level
control.

The resultsofthe pump drawdown testing are summarized in Table 2 below.

As previously stated, the existing pumps havearated maximum pumping capacity of 1,200 gpmatdesign system head conditions (130 ft TDH). The pump testing results appeartoindicate that the
actual system head under current operating conditions maybelower than the design system head.
Therefore, when pump speedissettofull speed (100% = 60 Hz), the pumps are operatingonthe
right-hand sideoftheir pump curve, thus pumpingahigher flow rate than their rated maximum
capacity (1,600-1,650 gpm vs. 1,200 gpm). As indicatedbypump testing data for Test No. 4, the
firm pumping capacityofthe pumping station appearstobe approximately 2,200 gpm when two
pumps run simultaneouslyinparallel, both operated at 100% pump speed.

The Town provided Pare withacopyofthe pump manufacturer’s operation and maintenance
manual, which contains factory performance test curves for the pumps. Following reviewofthis
information, it appears that the optimal pumping efficiency for this pump ( 80%) is achieved when
pumping approximately 1,400 gpm at 120-125 ft of TDH. At current pump speed settings (37 Hz),
the pumps are operatingata lower efficiencyofapproximately 55-57%. For comparison purposes, at
pump speeds of 45 Hz to 60 Hz, the pumps would operate between 72% and 80% efficiency, closertothe pumps’ optimal efficiency.

The pump testing indicates thatanincreaseinpump speed from 37 Hz (existing setting) to 45 Hz
would increase pumping flow ratebyapproximately 400 gpm. At the current wetwell operating
levels, this would increase pump starts per hour from 5 (existing normal flow conditions) to
approximately 6 starts per hour under normal flow conditions (based solelyonpump runtimes and
wetwell fill times recorded onsite during the testing). Thisisgenerally considered acceptable for
pump longevity and efficiency standards (6 pump starts per hourorless). Without adjustmentstowetwell operation levelsorpump speeds (manualorautomatic), pump runtimes and starts/stops
would increase and vary during periodsofincreased influent flow conditions, suchasduring and
following wet weather events.

-BW

## WORK REQUEST

On April 21, 2025, Harbor Controls participatedinpump testingatthe Bay Spring Ave Pump Station,
Barrington RI. The purposeofthe test wastocollect pump performance data when operating the
pumping systematvarious rates and pump pairings.

## BACKGROUD

The pump station has three sewage pumps which currently operateina lead / lag / standby
configuration. Pumps are started and stopped basedonthe levelinwet well. Pumps runata fixed
speed (operator adjustable) currently set to 62%.

## TEST PROCEEDURE

The following procedures were performed. Pare Engineering collected data for each test. The pump
starting levelinthe wet well was 45 inches and the pump stop level was 20 inches.

Operate each pump individuallyatcurrent speed setting, 62 %.
Operate each pump individually at 75%

Operate each pump individually at 100%

Operate pumpsinpairs, pump 1-2, 2-3 at 100%

control is 1) Manual speed, 2) Level Control. Manual speedistheir existing method. If Level controlisselected, the pump speed will vary basedonthe levelinthe wet well withinalevel operating band.

## TECHNICAL MEMORANDUM

DATE: April 7, 2025

TO: Charles Kalocsay — President
ShineHarmony Holdings, Inc.

FROM: _ Lisa Goyer, P.E. — Vice President
Brandon Blanchard, P.E. — Vice President

RE: Proposed Belton Court Village
Water Distribution System Hydraulic Evaluation
Barrington, RI
Pare Project No. 24258.00

At the request of ShineHarmony Holdings, Inc. (SHH), Pare Corporation (Pare) conductedanevaluationofthe existing drinking water distribution systematthe locationofthe proposed “Belton Court Village”
development, formerly the Zion Bible College Campus, in the Town of Barrington, RI (the Town). The
evaluation was completed using the distribution system hydraulic model developed in Bentley’s
WaterGems software. Pare understands that ShineHarmony Holdings, Inc. (SHH) is proposinganew

residential development containing 344 residential unitsonan approximate 37-acre plotofland, Town
Assessor’s Plat 14 Lots 002, 004, 013, 341, 342, and 343 locatedonthe west side of Middle Highway.

Background

The proposed developmentislocated within the Bristol County Water Authority’s (BCWA) main service
area. The pressureinthe main service areaismaintainedbythe hydraulic grade level (HGL) of the
Bayview water storage tank withanoverflow elevation of 182.7 (mean sea level). The ground elevationofthe proposed development ranges from 44 to 58. These elevations were obtained from an “Existing
Conditions” plan prepared by InSite Engineering Services, LLC (Insite - engineerofrecord for the
proposed development project) dated May 2, 2023. The limitsofthe proposed development and proposed
water system piping and BCWA connection point are depicted on Figure 1.

Per the Rhode Island Department of Health’s Rules and Regulations Pertaining to Public Drinking Water,
the designofwater treatment works, water storageorwater pumping facilities should reflect the guidance
contained in (Ten State Standards) Recommended Standards for Water Works: Policies for the Review
and Approval of Plans and Specifications for Public Water Supplies (most recent edition), where
applicable. These facilities shall alsobeinstalled and constructedinaccordance with applicable American
Water Works Association (AWWA) Standards with referencetomaterials used and construction
procedurestobe followed.

As stated in Section 8.2.1 of the Ten State Standards, all water mains, including those not designedtoprovide fire protection, shallbedesignedtomaintainaminimum pressure of 20 psiatground levelatall

pointsinthe distribution system under all conditionsofflow. The normal working pressureinthe
distribution system shouldbeapproximately 60 to 80 psi and not less than 35 psi.

Water System Demands
Pare obtained the following information for the proposed residential development from InSite.

Table 1 — Belton Court Village Proposed Residential Units

Pare used the projected wastewater flowstoestimate the water demands. For this evaluation, it was
assumed that wastewater flowisabout 90% of water consumption. The estimated wastewater flows were
calculatedinaccordance with the Onsite Wastewater Treatment System (OWTS) Rule (250-RICR-150-
10-6, Section 6.22.B: Wastewater Design Flows) defining the wastewater design flow foraresidential
singleormultiple family residence as 115 gallons per day (gpd) per bedroom. The wastewater design
flows providedinthe OWTS regulations are considered maximum day flow. Therefore, 128 gpd (115
gpd/0.9 = 128 gpd) per bedroom was usedtocalculate the maximum day water demand (MDD). Pare
estimated the total MDD for the proposed developmenttobe 97,152 gpd, or 67.5 gallons per minute

(gpm).

The development will also includeanapproximate 5,000 square-foot common areaorcommercial space.
The actual useofthis spaceisyettobe determined, so additional water demands were not considered.
This evaluation also did not consider demand that maybeproposed for irrigation.

Hydraulic Evaluation

To perform the model simulation, pipes and junctions were inserted into the BCWA computerized modeltorepresent the proposed developmentasdepicted on Figure 1. This includesaflow meter and backflow
deviceatthe proposed connectiontothe BCWA distribution system. To simulate the head losses through
the meter and backflow devices, manufacturer headloss curves for a 6-inch Neptune Protectus III Fire
Service Meter and a 6-inch Watts reduced pressure zone, Series LF909, backflow device were usedasmodel input. This meter was selected because the BCWA standardizes with Neptune meters and Watts
backflow preventers are commonly usedinthe industry. All the proposed piping was assumedtobe 8-
--- Page 588 ---

MmarE

## CORPORATION

Mr. Charles Kalocsay (3) April 7, 2025

inch cement-lined ductile iron pipe basedoninput from Insite and SHH. One connectiontothe water
distribution system was modeledtoreflect the anticipated connection requirements from BCWA.

Subsequently, initial settingsofthe tank water levels, pump status, and valve positions were adjustedtosimulate the current operationsofthe system. This included removing the Fountain Avenue Storage Tank
from operation, closing the Middle Highway pressure reducing valve (PRV), and setting the booster
pumpatthe Nayatt Avenue Pump Station to 50% speed.

To estimate the static pressure and available fire flows throughout the proposed development, the total
MDD of 67.5 gpm was distributed evenly throughout proposed development. This distribution was
estimated from our reviewofthe planned layout depicted on Insite’s “Preliminary Utility Layout Plan”.
The resulting static pressureatthe modeled junctions are listedinthe following table. Pressures ranged
from 48 to 54 psi which accounts foranapproximate 10 psi pressure drop through the meter and backflow
device.

Summary

It appears the BCWA water system can provide adequate domestic pressuretothe proposed development,
meeting the minimum required 35 psi. However, it does not meet normal working pressure of 60 to 80
psi. Pressures are estimatedtorange from 48 psi to 54 psi during a MDD condition.

Itisalso estimated that the system can provideanavailable fire flowofapproximately 1,000 gpmtothe
proposed development without causing the pressureinother partsofthe systemtofall below 20 psi.

## MEMORANDUM
DATE: May 16, 2025
TO: Barrington Planning Board/Town Planner Herbert Durfee III
FROM: Alan M. Corvi- Barrington DPw /Pué
RE: Proposed Belton Court Village — Off-Site Sanitary Sewer Capacity Analysis

In September 2024, InSite Engineering Services, LLC submitted designs plans (on behalf of ShineHarmony
Holdings, Inc.) for the developmentofthe former Zion Bible School at 33 Middle Highway. InSite had
designedaresidential development with 344 units withaproposed average day sewer flowofnearly 88,000
GPD and were requestingaletterofeligibilitytoconnecttothe Town’s sanitary sewer system. The
Department of Public Works had concerns with the proposed development and it’s potential impacttothe
Town's sanitary sewer system including the Bay Spring Pump Station. ShineHarmony Holdings brought in
Pare Corporationtoconductasanitary sewer capacity analysisofthe existing municipal sanitary sewer
collection system located immediately downstreamofthe former Zion Bible College campus inclusiveofthe
receiving Bay Spring Pump Station.

Basedonthe analysis, Pare Corporation has recommended that the three sections of 8-inch sewer pipe in
County Road (SMH 7-8, SMH 8-9 and SMH 9-10) have their capacity increasedbyreplacing them with larger
pipe (12 inch or 15 inch diameter) to accommodate the increased sanitary sewer flow from the proposed
development. The approximate lengthsofthese three sectionsofpipesisas follows: SMH 7-8 = 122 feet,
SMH 8-9 = 120 feet, and SMH 9-10 = 409 feet foracombined total of 651’ feetofpipe replacement. (see
Pare Technical Memorandum revised dated: May 6, 2025 by Brian Wrigley and Brandon Blanchard of Pare)
The 651 feet of 8-inch sanitary sewer main will needtobe upgradedtothe larger pipe (inclusiveofall
manhole work/replacementasrequired, design plans(approvedbythe Town)/utility permits, police details,
construction inspection, excavation, dewatering, by-passingofthe sewer flowtokeep constant operation
without disruption, all materials, pipe and manholes, all roadway restorationtothe satisfaction of RIDOT
inclusiveofall hot mix asphalt paving (temporary & permanent), traffic line markings replacement, loam and
seed, sidewalk restoration/curb resetasneeded, as-built planofall work and any incidentals needtocompete
the worktothe satisfactionofthe Town. This work shouldbecompeted by ShineHarmonyattheir expense

priortooratthe same time that the new development’s connectionismade into the Town’s sanitary sewer
system.

Pare Corporation has also noted that duetothe significant increaseinsanitary sewer flow (from this proposed
development) to the Bay Spring Pump Station, ShineHarmony Holdings will assist the Town with identifying
the modificationstopump operations that are necessarytoaccommodate this increaseinflow. Therefore,
Pare Corporation (working through/for ShineHarmony) should direct/guide the Town with the necessary
modificationstothe Bay Spring Pump Station operations with all expenses covered by ShineHarmony.

InSite Engineeringisproposing that the sanitary sewer from the new development exit the site through the
easement to Great Road. ShineHarmony should have their engineer ensure that this pipe system can
accommodate the flowofthis development and also ensure that this existing pipe and manhole systemisin

good operating condition and does not need upgrading. Any needed improvementstothis sewer main willbeat the expense of ShineHarmony.

Therefore, the Department of Public Worksisrequesting that ShineHarmony Holdings address the above-
noted itemsasconditionsofapproval for connectionofthe proposed developmenttothe Town’s off-site
sanitary sewer system. This approval memorandumisfor only the sanitary sewer connectiontothe
Town system and that all other review/comment and approval for the on-site work (roadway design,
on-site sanitary sewer design/details, grading, drainage, etc.) willbeat later submissions.

Description of Proposal: Seeking approval for Preliminary Plan and relief from
provisionsofthe Zoning Ordinance through the Comprehensive Permit Process.
Comprehensive Permit for property commonly known as Belton Court, 33 Middle
Highway, AP14, Lots 2, 4, 13, 341, 342, and 343, Senior Residential Village District.

e E. Any requested waiversormodificationofrequirementsaswritten in

waivers

Tobeclearer than the original Notice of Incomplete Application, the Low & Moderate
Income Housing Act includes definitions [RIGL § 45-53-3]. The term:

(1) “Adjustment(s)” [aka “waiver(s)”] meansarequestorrequestsbythe applicanttoseek relief from the literal use and dimensional requirementsofthe municipal
zoning ordinance and/or the design standardsorrequirementsofthe municipal land
development and subdivision regulations. The standard for the local review board's

considerationofadjustmentsisset forth in § 45-53-4(e)(3) (iii) (E) (I).

RIGL § 45-53-4(e)(3)(iii)(E), referenced above, states that the Planning Board must makeafindingonsix specified standards including the following:

(I) Whether the proposed developmentisin compliance with the standards and
provisionsofthe municipality's zoning ordinance and subdivision regulations,
and/or where adjustments [aka “waivers”’] are requestedbythe applicant, whether
local concerns that have been affectedbythe relief. ‘granteddonot outweigh the state

and local need for low- and moderate-income housing...

The Planning Board cannot makeareasonable findingofcompliance without havingalistofspecific waivers the applicant requirestocarry out the projectascurrently
proposed. In the applicant’s original submittal, the applicant offered an “... understandingofthe key modificationstothe current Residential Village Zoning District that wouldberequiredtoaccommodate the developmentofthe project...”

As partofthe applicant’s statement above, the applicant offeredalistofchanges the
Town needstomaketoits Zoning Ordinancetoaccommodate the development. Offering

this informationisnotinthe format required for requestingawaiver. Instead, to ensure
the onus lies with the applicant, the applicant needstospecifically cite the sectionofthe
Town Code, paraphrase the regulatory language from the Town Code, given that
regulation list what reliefisnecessary, and offeranexplanation why local concerns
affectedbyany relief granted will not outweigh the state and local need for low- and

moderate-income housing.

was granted for future reference (e.g., for title searches, any plan amendments, possible

## FURTHER RESPONSE TO CHECKLIST PARA. 4(E)

The required adjustments and waivers are set forth below, and repeatedonthe revised
Plan Set submitted separately herewith.

The reason / rationale for eachofthe requested adjustments / waiversisidentical, andisas follows:

The purposeofthe Low and Moderate Income Housing Act (R.I. Gen. Laws §45-53-1, ef
seq.) (the "Act”) istopromote use and design flexibilityinconnection with land use regulatory
requirements that are not essential for public health and safetyorenvironmental protection, and
that are not unreasonable and are consistent with local housing needs, and the aspirationsinthe
community's State-approved affordable housing plan, an elementofthe community's
Comprehensive Plan. Whereas, at one time the Act had required adjustments / waiverstobe
based on “impossibility” and/or "infeasibility", thatisno longer the case. Rather, adjustments /
waivers are now much more generally and flexibly available under the Act, as longasthey “do
not outweigh the state and local need for low and moderate income housing”. R.I. Gen. Laws
§ 45-53-4(d)(3)(iii MEI). Thatisa findingtobe madebythe Planning Board following the

Preliminary Plan hearing process.

The Act itself includes a “finding” and legislative statement of “intent” to the effect that
"there existsanacute shortageofaffordable, accessible, safe, and sanitary housing for [the
State's] citizensoflow and moderate income, both individuals and families, thatitis imperative
that actionistaken immediatelytoassure the availabilityofaffordable, accessible, safe, and
sanitary housing, for these persons”. And that "the provisionsofthis [Act] — including its liberal
approachtoadjustments/waivers ~ “are necessarytoassure the health, safety, and welfareofall
the citizensofthis state”.

genuine risktopublic health, safety, or welfare and are reasonably relatedtothe establishmentofhigh density multi-generational residential housing (i¢., housing not limitedtosenior citizens)
onasite that has been singled out within the Affordable Housing Planasoneofthe only
remaining available — and suitable — sites (in termsofsize, location, availabilityofpublic
utilities, etc.) to accomplish the Town's affordable housing objectives.

As set forthinfullinthe "Statement of Consistency With Comprehensive Plan” thatisincludedaspartofthe applicant's overall submission (Supplement #1 To Tab 9), oneofthe two
most important land use issues facing the Town is, “addingtothe affordable housing stock”.

(p. 133). The Comprehensive Plan specifically identifies the subject site here ~ the “former Zion
Bible Institute company on Middle Highway“ as oneofonly two properties in Town that are
suitabletoimplement creationofa compact “village” community witharangeofhousing types

family homesintraditional subdivisions may not meet the needsofall segmentsofthe
population ... ”. The Comprehensive Plan actually discourages exclusively “senior”
--- Page 603 ---

developments and prefers the diverse, multigenerational design proposed here. ("Rather than
encouraging segregationofthe elderlyinseparate retirement institutions, urban designers should
factor this social integration into more lively communities". (id., p. 17).

The vision articulatedinthe Comprehensive Plan requiresaretrofitting/retro-planningofwhat had beenaprivate campus with multiple dormitories and other buildings allinclose
proximity, established for mixed educational and residential purposes, withamultigenerational
planned unit community development/campus, allinclose proximity, with private ways, roads,
parking areas, and carefully planned attractive landscaping. The existing zoning and land
development dimensional and design regulations in Barrington, as described below, were not
built for suchaninnovative, uniquely crafted, approachinanyofthe existing zoning districts.
While the existing zoning district's emphasisonsenior housing would generally and
approximately accommodate overall density proposed here, more site specific design planningisneeded for the overall objective here — as outlined above — tobeaccommodated. The
adjustments / waivers, whichdonot involve genuine health / safety, welfare issues are integral
componentsofan overall design that harmoniously accomplishes the aforesaid goalsina way
that clearly outweighs the combined rigidities (outlined below) ofalocal regulatory
zoning/design structure that was not geared toward the objectivesofthe Actorthe local housing

needsofthe Town of Barrington.

Income Housing Act. At the Preliminary Plan stage the applicantisrequiredtoprovide
only, " ... Those items requiredbylocal regulations promulgated pursuanttoapplicable
state law, with the exceptionofevidenceofstateorfederal permits ...". (RI Gen. Laws
§ 45-53-4(d)(2)(i)(D); See also, § 45-53-4(d)(3(i)(A) ("All required state and federal
permits mustbeobtained priortothe final plan approvalorthe issuanceofa building
permit.")

Itisnoted that the Town of Barrington Comprehensive Planisexpired. However, to the
extent relevant under controlling State law, the proposalisconsistent with the most recent
Comprehensive Plan (2015 Update).

* The Land Use Elementofthe Comprehensive Plan update sets forth, in its very first
paragraph, on its first page (p. 133 overall) the most important land use issue for the Town: "As
Barrington approaches build-out, with few parcels that can support growth, the Townisfacing
critical issues integrally relatedtoland use planning." The first and foremost such issue set forth
was two headed: "Providing housing options foranaging population and addingtothe
affordable housing stock."

* The Land Use Element makes specific referencetothe subject property here, the former
Zion Bible Campusasa recourse that has the potentialtoadd over 200 units (p. 137). "Special
Approvals" on special properties like the Zion Bible Institute Campus alone were projectedtoincrease housing development by 590 units (above and beyond 255 additional single family
units, 70 multifamily units, and 112 residential demolitionsinthe normal course) from 2015 to

2035. (id., Table 6).

« The Land Use Element re-identified Zion Bible Institute Campus (7.e., repeating similar
referencesinthe 2009 Comprehensive Plan), "asasite uniqueintermsofsize, with almost 40
acres, and becauseofthe fact that the siteisin transition with the buildings mostly vacant after
the Zion Bible Institute vacated the premises.". (id., 145).

* The Land Use Element aptly elaborated that, "Communities with ample land for future
growth often plan for large areas involving many property owners; however, becauseofthe
limited amountofland left, the Zion siteisidentified specificallyasan opportunity area for
meeting multiple land use objectives.

« The Land Use Element further references and re-incorporatesadocument entitled,
"Appendix II; Developer Guidance", originallyapartofthe State-approved 2010
Comprehensive Plan — that "providesameanstoevaluate proposed development projects and/or
proposing new zoning for the site." (id., pp. 145, 146).

¢ The "Developer Guidance — Appendix I" to the 2015 Comprehensive Plan Update states
that, "Two sites have been identified, through a 2008 Housing Land Use Study, as candidates for
future village development: the 40-acre Zion Bible Institute, and the Sowams Nursery property.

"

» The Developer Guidance — Appendix II" to the 2015 Comprehensive Plan Update
contains specific findingsasfollows:
--- Page 622 ---

"2 Findings

A. The Town of Barrington's Comprehensive Community Plan outlines strategiestoachieve the state-mandated 10 percent low and moderate income housing goal,
and expanding housing options for current and future residents of Barrington.

The Comprehensive Plan identifiesaneedtoprovide housing foragrowing
senior population, workforce housing for municipal employees and other workers,
and persons with disabilities. (id.).

B. Two specific areasofthe Town identifiedinthe Housing For Barrington's
Future: Housing Land Use Study are suitabletoimplement creationofa compact
"village" community witharangeofhousing typesatvarying densities". The
former Zion Bible Institute campus on Middle Highway (Plat 14 / Lots 2, 4, 13,
341, 342, 343 and the former Sowam's Nursery.

¢ The proposed project designisalso compatible with the key design guidelinesofthe
"Appendix IT Developer Guidance", including: H (Walkability); I (Social Integration);
K (retainingaportion of Belton Court), and N (Mixed use).

¢ As notedinthe Plan, a purposeofincluding such sitesinthe Plan is, "to achieve the
following: A. Increase diversityofhousing types in Barrington, with dwelling units that meet
the needsofresidentsatvarying life stages, recognizing that single family homesintraditional
subdivisions may not meet the needsofall segmentsofthe population, especially the senior
population.” (id.).

¢ The "Senior Residential Village" and "Residential Village" categoriesonthe Future
Land Use Mapinthe 2015 Update are both described (in termsofproposed development) as
follows:

"Master-planned developments for large multi-acre tracts that incorporateawide
varietyofland usesasdescribedinthe developer guidance for establishmentofthese zoning districts (Appendix IT)".

Thisisthe designationofthe Subject Propertyinthe Plan.

* The 2015 Update recitedinits "Housing And Neighborhoods" element that Barrington
was 467 units shortofthe 10 percent affordable housing goal. (id., p. 20).

¢ The 2015 Update also underscores the reality that, "with much of Barrington essentiallyatbuildout", itisimportanttodetermine, "how will Barrington meet future needs for housing,
both affordable and market rate, given the limited supplyofdevelopable properties that have
accesstowater and sewer and adequate roadstosupport this growth?" (id., 6).

... "As depicted on Map HN-1, the remaining vacant buildable parcels — zoned residentialorcommercial — are scattered throughout Barrington, with justafew with more than 5 acres of
--- Page 623 ---

developable upland that also have accesstoinfrastructure necessarytosupport new development
(water and sewer)." (id., 9).

* The 2015 Update articulated an "Objective" (Objective HN-3.2: "Produce affordable
housing unitsata rate that keeps Barringtononthe path toward achieving the 10 percent
affordable goal by 2035.")

At that time the Town was at 2.6% of the 10% goal. Accordingtothe Plan, this meant
Barrington was 467 units shortofthe 10% affordable housing goal. (id., p. 20). (The Town had,
per census, 6,386 dwelling unitsatthe time.) However, also Accordingtothe Plan, factoringinadditional growth, including additional market rate units, "Barrington would needtoproduce
approximately 544 more affordable unitstomeet the 10 percent standard over the next 20 years

[i.e., by 2035]. (id., p. 15.

- At this point, roughly half way through the 20 years, the Townisstill 373 units shortofthe 10% goal, not accounting for future growth. This means thatifno additional market rate
dwellingisbuilt in Barringtoninthe next 10 years, it would still needtoaveragealittle over 37
new affordable housing units each yeartoreach the goal. (HousingWorks RI at Roger Williams
University 2025 Housing Fact Book).

- And the Subject Property remains the only property left that was identifiedinthe 2015
Updateasa suitable site for higher density multifamily housing. (The Sowams Nursery propertyisalready spoken for.)

¢ Barrington's current affordable housing needs are grave. 1,571 of its households are
"cost burdened" (1,243 Owner Households [23% of all Owner Households]); and 328 Rental
Households, 57% of all Rental Households. ("Cost Burdened" meansahouseholdisspending
more than 30% of its incomeonhousing costs.) Total households are currently 5,978. 89%
own; 11% rent. (HousingWorks RI at Roger Williams University 2025 Housing Fact Book).

* The Comprehensive Plan discourages exclusively "senior" developments and prefers the
multi-generational design proposed. The Plan states, "Rather than encouraging segregationofthe elderlyinseparate retirement institutions, urban designers should factor this social
integration into more lively communities." (id., p. 17.
